lore: The Lesser Redpoll is a small finch that winters in Amsterdam parks and tree-lined canals, often in flocks feeding on alder and birch catkins. It clings acrobatically to slender twigs in the upper canopy and flits short distances between trees, exactly matching the small-songbird default. Around the city it is most easily seen in Vondelpark, Oosterpark, and the Amsterdamse Bos where mature Alnus and Betula stand together. The standard tree-canopy / tree-flitter combination fits without modification.

The hawthorn shieldbug feeds on the developing fruits and leaves of Crataegus and related rosaceous trees, and is regularly recorded on hawthorn hedges and isolated trees across Amsterdam parks. It is a slow-moving, sit-and-suck feeder rather than a flower-visiting pollinator, so the insect default of flower-visitor / flower-bobber does not fit. Adults overwinter and emerge to bask on sunlit foliage in spring. Tree-canopy + idle-only better captures its real on-leaf, mostly-stationary behavior.

These are tiny mites that graze sooty mould and fungal films on the leaves and bark of honeydew-laden trees like Liriodendron, Ulmus and Zelkova. They are essentially invisible to the naked eye and effectively stationary at the renderer's scale, so the arachnid default of anywhere / idle-only collapses neatly to tree-canopy in this tree-feeding context. The reason for override is purely to pin them to their host trees rather than scatter them across all habitats.

Hackberry gall mites induce small pouch and nipple galls on the leaves of Celtis trees, which are increasingly used as a heat-tolerant street tree in Amsterdam. The mites are microscopic and entirely sedentary inside galls. As with other eriophyid groupings, the override pins them to Celtis canopy rather than the arachnid anywhere default.

These eriophyid mites form the classic red bead-galls on sycamore and field maple leaves, common on Acer pseudoplatanus and A. campestre throughout Amsterdam. The galls are highly visible but the mites themselves never leave them. Tree-canopy + idle-only fits; override is to anchor placement to the host Acer rather than the arachnid default.

Eriophyid gall mites on Carpinus produce the small velvety erinea and leaf-margin rolls familiar on hornbeam in Amsterdam street plantings and park hedges. They live their entire life cycle inside leaf galls and never move at any visible scale. Tree-canopy + idle-only is the correct placement; the override exists so they sit on their Carpinus host rather than the arachnid generic anywhere default.

The sycamore moth's striking yellow-and-black hairy caterpillar feeds on Acer leaves in mid to late summer and is occasionally found on horse chestnut and oak. In Amsterdam it turns up on park and street maples once larvae are large enough to spot. The adult is a night-flying moth not typically seen, so the grouping is dominated by the visible larval stage on foliage: tree-canopy + idle-only rather than the flower-visitor insect default.

A grouping of alder-feeding moth caterpillars including the alder moth and the small eggar, whose communal silk tents are sometimes spotted on alder trees along Amsterdam's canal-side plantings. The visible life stage is the foliage-feeding larva, often resting in conspicuous postures. Tree-canopy + idle-only fits this larva-dominant grouping better than the pollinator-shaped insect default.

Aculus mosoniensis is an eriophyid mite that has spread across Europe on tree-of-heaven (Ailanthus altissima), causing leaf curling and witches' broom symptoms. Ailanthus is widespread as a self-sown street and ruderal tree in Amsterdam, so this mite is common wherever the host stands. As with all eriophyids, microscopic and sedentary; tree-canopy + idle-only.

Spruce gall adelgids form distinctive pineapple-shaped galls at the base of new shoots on Picea, particularly P. abies in Amsterdam park plantings and cemeteries. The sap-feeding insects live their winged life cycle largely inside galls or sessile on needles. Tree-canopy + idle-only is appropriate; flower-visitor / flower-bobber would be completely wrong for a needle-galling adelgid.

The larch woolly adelgid is a sap-sucking insect that produces fluffy white wax on Larix needles; the related life cycle alternates onto spruce. In Amsterdam Larix is uncommon but present in older park plantings such as the Amsterdamse Bos. The visible stage is sessile and tree-feeding, so tree-canopy + idle-only overrides the flower-visitor default.

The alder leaf beetle is a small metallic-blue chrysomelid that skeletonises alder leaves; outbreaks are very visible on canal-side and park alders across Amsterdam. Adults and larvae are both leaf-bound feeders rather than flower visitors. Tree-canopy + idle-only captures their on-leaf existence better than the pollinator default; adults can short-fly between alders but most of the visible life is at rest on foliage.

Agrilus difficilis is a metallic wood-boring beetle whose larvae mine under the bark of Gleditsia triacanthos, a common Amsterdam street tree. Adults emerge through D-shaped exit holes and bask on trunks and branches in summer. The visible stage is on bark; tree-bark + idle-only fits better than the flower-visitor insect default for a buprestid borer.

The spiny whitefly is an invasive sap-feeding hemipteran that has been spreading on Ailanthus and citrus in southern Europe and has reached the Netherlands as a quarantine pest. On Amsterdam's Ailanthus it produces honeydew and sooty mould on leaf undersides. Larvae are sessile; adults are weak flutterers but largely stay on the host. Tree-canopy + idle-only overrides the pollinator default.

Andrena mining bees are a diverse genus of solitary spring-active pollinators that nest in bare or sparsely vegetated soil and forage on early-blooming trees and shrubs — exactly the host list here: Amelanchier, Salix, Sorbus, Laburnum, Koelreuteria, Paulownia. They are abundant in Amsterdam parks, cemeteries, and unmown verges during March–May. The flower-visitor / flower-bobber default fits without modification.

The apple blossom weevil lays eggs inside unopened Malus flower buds; the larva develops inside, killing the bud and leaving the characteristic capped-blossom symptom on ornamental and wild apples in Amsterdam parks and orchards. Although the adult does visit flowers briefly, the visible life of the species is on apple buds and shoots. Tree-canopy + idle-only better reflects its anchored-on-host behavior than the generic pollinator default.

Anthophora and Osmia are fast-flying solitary bees that visit Laburnum and other spring-flowering trees in Amsterdam; Osmia bicornis is particularly common on bee hotels in city gardens. They hover and bob between blooms in classic pollinator fashion. The flower-visitor / flower-bobber default fits cleanly.

This grouping combines two large woodland butterflies whose larvae feed on Salix (Purple Emperor on willow, White Admiral on honeysuckle but often near willow). Adults rarely visit flowers — Purple Emperor is famous for ignoring blooms and instead descending to ground for minerals or perching high in willow canopies. Both spend most visible time aloft in tree canopy, so tree-canopy + flower-bobber (for general adult flight) better matches the dominant member, Apatura iris.

Hazel-feeding aphids including the large hazel aphid (Corylobium avellanae) and the common hazel aphid (Myzocallis coryli), found on Corylus throughout Amsterdam parks and ornamental plantings. Both are sap-feeders that cluster on leaves and shoots. Tree-canopy + idle-only is the correct grouping override.

Hawthorn-feeding aphids including the hawthorn-carrot aphid and the hawthorn-mint aphid, on Crataegus monogyna and ornamental hawthorns throughout Amsterdam's hedgerows and street tree plantings. Sap-feeding, leaf-curling, sessile aphid colonies. Tree-canopy + idle-only.

Generalist greenfly aphids occasionally recorded on Metasequoia glyptostroboides, a deciduous conifer planted as an ornamental in several Amsterdam parks. The aphids are sessile sap-feeders on young shoots. Tree-canopy + idle-only override consistent with other aphid groupings in this batch.

Cornus-feeding aphids including Anoecia corni, the dogwood-grass aphid that alternates between dogwood (primary host) and grass roots. Common on the many ornamental Cornus in Amsterdam parks and gardens. Tree-canopy + idle-only is the appropriate override.

Aphids on Morus (mulberry) including the highly polyphagous cotton/melon aphid Aphis gossypii. Mulberry is a niche but present street and garden tree in Amsterdam. Standard aphid override: tree-canopy + idle-only.

Ginkgo biloba is famously aphid-resistant, but occasional aphid records exist, mostly vagrants from nearby host trees. In Amsterdam Ginkgo is a popular ornamental street tree. Tree-canopy + idle-only override as for all aphid groupings.

A catch-all grouping of generalist aphids and soft scale insects that feed on the foliage and young shoots of a wide range of Amsterdam street and park trees: Ailanthus, Catalpa, Liquidambar, Populus, Pterocarya, and many more listed. They are sessile to slow-walking sap feeders that produce honeydew and sooty mould. Tree-canopy + idle-only fits the visible life stage; the flower-visitor default does not apply.

Aphis is a large genus of generalist aphids feeding on many woody hosts including Paulownia and Styphnolobium, both planted as ornamental street trees in Amsterdam. Sessile sap-feeders on leaves and tender shoots. Tree-canopy + idle-only.

The alder spittlebug nymph produces the conspicuous frothy "cuckoo spit" on alder shoots in early summer; adults are leafhopper-shaped and rest on foliage. Common on alders along Amsterdam waterways and in park plantings. Tree-canopy + idle-only fits the visible nymph and resting-adult stages better than the pollinator default.

A combined grouping of honeybees, bumblebees, and Andrena mining bees visiting Ostrya carpinifolia (hop-hornbeam), an uncommon but planted ornamental in Amsterdam. The grouping is the textbook pollinator assemblage. Flower-visitor / flower-bobber default fits without modification.

Apidae here covers honeybees (Apis mellifera) and early-emerging bumblebees (Bombus terrestris, B. pascuorum) that work the catkins of Corylus and the inflorescences of Morus in Amsterdam parks and gardens. They are the canonical flower-visiting pollinators the insect default was designed for. No override needed.

Apiognomonia veneta is the causal agent of plane anthracnose, a widespread fungal disease of Platanus (plane trees) in Amsterdam's urban canals and parks. The fungus colonises bark, twigs, and leaf scars, overwintering in necrotic tissue on the trunk and branches. In spring it releases conidia during wet conditions, causing dieback of new shoots and blight of emerging leaves. The tree-bark placement is appropriate because the pathogen's fruiting bodies (acervuli in the asexual Discula stage) emerge directly from infected bark tissue rather than soil or canopy foliage. It is one of the most commonly observed diseases on Amsterdam's many mature plane avenues.

This combined entry covers honey bees and bumblebees observed on Pterocarya (Caucasian wingnut) in Amsterdam. Pterocarya is a large riparian tree sometimes planted along Amsterdam's waterways; its catkins offer pollen to early-season generalist bees. Both Apis and Bombus are common flower visitors across Amsterdam's urban green spaces, moving rapidly between flowering trees and shrubs. The flower-visitor habitat and flower-bobber movement class are the correct defaults for these pollinators: their map placement should snap to nearby flowering tree sprites and animate with short inter-flower bobs.

This grouped entry covers honey bees (Apis mellifera) alongside solitary mining bees (Andrena spp.) observed on Malus (crabapple / ornamental apple) in Amsterdam. Malus is a popular street and park tree whose spring blossoms attract dense concentrations of both managed honey bees and numerous ground-nesting Andrena species. Andrena bees nest in bare soil patches in parks but forage almost exclusively on flowers, so the flower-visitor habitat is the most visible and meaningful placement. The shared flower-bobber movement class captures both species foraging behavior accurately during the blooming season.

Apis mellifera, the western honey bee, is one of Amsterdam's most widely observed pollinators and appears on 20 different tree genera in this dataset — the broadest tree association of any single species in the batch. It forages on everything from Tilia (lime) to Robinia (black locust) to Magnolia and Salix, covering virtually every flowering street tree in the city. Amsterdam supports managed hive colonies on rooftops and in parks, and feral colonies in tree cavities. The flower-visitor placement and flower-bobber animation are ideal: honey bees are most visible on blooming canopy trees and move with rapid, purposeful inter-flower hops. Out of bloom season they are effectively invisible, which the flower-visitor fallback logic handles correctly.

This entry groups honey bees (Apis mellifera) and bumblebees (Bombus spp.) found on Acer, Gleditsia, and Ilex in Amsterdam. Both genera are generalist pollinators highly abundant in Amsterdam's parks, canal-side plantings, and gardens. They forage actively among flowering trees and shrubs, making short bobbing transits between blooms and returning to hive or nest between foraging bouts. The flower-visitor habitat and flower-bobber movement defaults are a perfect fit: these insects are most conspicuous when working a flowering tree canopy and largely invisible when underground or in the hive. Amsterdam populations benefit from the city's diverse street tree flora and the many small gardens.

This grouped entry combines wood mice and bank voles (ground-dwelling small rodents) with squirrels (partly arboreal) observed on Fagus (beech) in Amsterdam's parks. The dominant habitat is ground-park because mice and voles forage primarily on the forest floor and among root systems, but the tree-canopy secondary reflects the squirrels' regular use of the canopy for movement and caching. Similarly, park-roamer is the dominant movement for the nocturnal rodents while tree-flitter captures squirrel movement between branches. The combination is justified because this slug groups ecologically distinct animals; the dominant tags reflect the mice/voles which likely outnumber squirrels in observations.

This three-species group is observed on Corylus (hazel) in Amsterdam, a plant whose nuts are heavily exploited by all three mammals. Wood mice and bank voles forage primarily on the ground, caching hazel nuts in shallow burrows, while red squirrels (Sciurus vulgaris) spend significant time in the canopy leaping between hazel stems. Red squirrel is rare in central Amsterdam but present in larger suburban parks and Amsterdamse Bos. The dual habitat tags reflect the genuine split between ground-foraging rodents (dominant) and the arboreal squirrel, with tree-flitter as a meaningful secondary movement for the squirrel component.

This combined entry groups the wood mouse (Apodemus sylvaticus) and bank vole (Myodes glareolus) found on Cornus, Crataegus, and Ostrya in Amsterdam. Both species are small nocturnal ground mammals that shelter under shrubs and fallen wood, foraging on seeds, berries, and invertebrates. Bank voles are especially associated with scrubby undergrowth near water, while wood mice range through park interiors. Neither species is meaningfully arboreal despite their association with fruiting trees; ground-park placement and park-roamer movement are the accurate defaults. Their tree associations here reflect seed-predation on fallen fruit from Cornus and Crataegus rather than canopy use.

The wood mouse (Apodemus sylvaticus) and the closely related yellow-necked mouse (A. flavicollis) are common small rodents in Amsterdam's Vondelpark, Amsterdamse Bos, and larger cemetery parks. They are associated here with Prunus (cherry / plum) whose fallen fruits and seeds form a major part of their diet in autumn. Both species are nocturnal ground-foragers, living in burrows or dense undergrowth and rarely visible; the ground-park habitat and park-roamer movement default are accurate. They occasionally climb low branches to reach fruit but this is incidental and does not warrant a secondary tag.

Apoidea is the superfamily encompassing all bees (honey bees, bumblebees, solitary bees), recorded here on Cornus (dogwood) in Amsterdam. Cornus mas and C. sanguinea flower early in spring, offering some of the season's first pollen to queen bumblebees and early-flying solitary bees. Amsterdam's diverse urban planting provides year-round foraging niches for bee communities. The flower-visitor placement and flower-bobber movement are the appropriate category defaults for this superfamily grouping: all member species are most meaningfully placed at flowering trees and animated with inter-bloom transits.

Appendiseta robiniae (the locust aphid or black locust aphid) is a specialist phloem-feeding aphid restricted to Robinia pseudoacacia (black locust), which is common in Amsterdam's parks and along canal-side plantings. Colonies form dense aggregations on the undersides of leaves and on young shoots in the tree canopy from spring through summer. Like all tree-canopy aphids, individuals are effectively immobile on their host — wingless morphs especially so — making idle-only the correct movement class. The tree-canopy habitat override from the insect default (flower-visitor) applies because this is a tree-feeding microorganism on Robinia rather than a pollinator.

This grouped entry covers spiders, harvestmen, and woodlice (Isopoda — crustaceans, technically not arachnids) found on Cupressocyparis (Leyland cypress) in Amsterdam. Leyland cypress has dense, fibrous bark that shelters a community of bark-dwelling invertebrates: spiders hunting in the crevices, harvestmen resting by day, and woodlice feeding on decaying bark tissue. The tree-bark habitat override from the arachnid default (anywhere) is appropriate here because the observation context is specifically bark microhabitat. Idle-only movement fits all three groups: woodlice creep too slowly to animate distinctly, and the spiders and harvestmen are mostly stationary unless disturbed.

This general Arachnida entry covers spiders and harvestmen found on Ginkgo in Amsterdam. Ginkgo biloba is a popular Amsterdam street tree, and its broad canopy provides habitat for a variety of generalist web-building and wandering spiders as well as harvestmen (Opiliones) that shelter among leaf litter and bark crevices. Arachnida as a class is far too ecologically diverse for a specific habitat assignment — canopy orb-weavers, ground-hunting wolf spiders, and bark-dwelling jumping spiders are all present — so the anywhere default with idle-only movement is the correct catch-all. Per-species overrides would be appropriate if individual species were identified.

This unusual entry describes the community of overwintering invertebrates sheltering in the dead but persistent (marcescent) leaves of Carpinus (hornbeam), Cercidiphyllum, and Chamaecyparis in Amsterdam. Marcescent leaves on these trees retain their foliage through winter, providing insulation for dormant spiders, true bugs (Hemiptera), and beetles (Coleoptera). The dominant taxon listed is Araneae so the arachnid category is most appropriate, though this is a mixed community. Tree-canopy placement reflects the fact that the leaves hang on the tree rather than on the ground; idle-only movement is correct for dormant, overwintering animals making no voluntary movements until spring warmth returns.

Araneidae (orb-weavers) and Theridiidae (cobweb spiders) are generalist web-building spiders that construct webs in the canopy of Ailanthus altissima (tree of heaven) in Amsterdam. Ailanthus is a vigorous coloniser of disturbed urban sites and its open branching architecture makes it an ideal frame for spider webs. These spiders spend most of their time stationary at the hub of their webs waiting for prey, making idle-only the correct movement class. Tree-canopy overrides the arachnid default (anywhere) because the observation context is specifically canopy web habitat; this is a more precise and placement-useful tag than the generic fallback.

Argynnis fritillary butterflies (silver-washed fritillary A. paphia and high brown fritillary A. adippe) are large, powerful woodland-edge butterflies that would be unusual in central Amsterdam but may occur in the Amsterdamse Bos and larger wooded parks. They are associated with Fraxinus (ash) here, whose canopy shade creates the dappled woodland edge conditions these species prefer. Their primary habitat is flower-visitor when foraging on bramble, thistles, and other blooms, but they also glide and rest at ground level in grassy clearings, warranting the ground-park secondary. The park-roamer secondary captures the territorial gliding flights these large fritillaries make through woodland glades.

Argyresthia conjugella, the apple fruit moth, is a small moth whose larvae tunnel into the fruits and seeds of Sorbus (rowan) and Malus (apple) in Amsterdam. The larvae develop entirely inside the fruit, feeding on the flesh and seeds, then drop to the soil to pupate. Adults are tiny (wingspan ~10mm), silvery-white moths that rest on foliage by day. The primary ecological interaction — the larval mining stage — occurs within the tree canopy on the fruit, making tree-canopy the appropriate habitat override. Idle-only reflects the larval immobility within the fruit mine; this is a tree-feeding microorganism on Sorbus.

Argyresthia tip moths associated with Cupressocyparis and Thuja in Amsterdam are small tortricid-like moths whose larvae mine into the growing tips (shoots) of cypress and arborvitae, causing the characteristic brown dead-tip dieback visible on these trees across Dutch gardens and parks. The larvae live entirely within the shoot tissue and the adults, when present, rest cryptically on foliage. Tree-canopy is the correct habitat because both larval and adult stages are anchored to the tree's foliage tips rather than the bark or ground. Idle-only movement reflects the larval stage's complete immobility within the mine and the adult's resting behavior; this is a tree-feeding microorganism on Cupressocyparis/Thuja.

The Hackberry Emperor (Asterocampa celtis) is a North American butterfly whose sole larval host is Celtis (hackberry), the same tree genus present in Amsterdam as an introduced species. This butterfly is native to North America and is not established in Amsterdam or Europe; its inclusion in the dataset reflects an observation tied to the Celtis tree host rather than an established Amsterdam population. Its ecology: adults glide and perch high in hackberry canopy, rarely visiting flowers (they prefer tree sap, rotting fruit, and carrion). Tree-canopy is the most accurate habitat; flower-bobber is retained from the insect default as the closest available movement class for a butterfly, though this species is atypically non-floral.

Atteva aurea, the ailanthus webworm moth, is a brightly colored ermine-like moth native to tropical America that has spread into Europe alongside its secondary host Ailanthus altissima (tree of heaven). In Amsterdam, Ailanthus colonises disturbed urban sites and wasteland; the webworm moth larvae feed communally in silk webs on Ailanthus foliage through summer. The adults are day-active and striking — orange with white and black spots — but rest on foliage rather than foraging at flowers. Tree-canopy is the correct habitat override because larvae live and feed in canopy webs; idle-only reflects their sedentary larval feeding behavior. This is a tree-feeding organism on Ailanthus.

This entry groups the barbastelle bat (Barbastella barbastellus), a rare and declining species in the Netherlands, with the common pipistrelle (Pipistrellus pipistrellus and relatives), which are abundant across Amsterdam foraging over canals, parks, and street-lit edges after dark. Both species are associated with Salix (willow) here, which provides roost sites in cavity-rich old willows along Amsterdam's waterways. Bats are aerial hunters that spend their active time in flight, making sky-only habitat and sky-looper movement the clear overrides from the mammal default (ground-park / park-roamer). They roost in tree cavities by day but their map presence should represent them in flight over open water and park polygons.

Blepharidopterus chlorionis is a plant bug (family Miridae) that feeds on Gleditsia triacanthos (honey locust), increasingly planted as a street tree in Amsterdam due to its salt and drought tolerance. This small green-brown bug feeds on leaf tissue and is found on the undersides of leaflets from early summer. Like most Miridae, it is cryptically colored and relatively sedentary on its host plant, making tree-canopy the correct habitat override and idle-only the appropriate movement class. This is a tree-feeding microorganism on Gleditsia; the insect default of flower-visitor does not apply to a specialized plant bug.

Bombus terrestris (buff-tailed bumblebee) and B. hortorum (garden bumblebee) are two of Amsterdam's most common and conspicuous bumblebee species, observed here on Aesculus, Amelanchier, Koelreuteria, Laburnum, Paulownia, and Styphnolobium — a rich set of flowering street and park trees. B. terrestris is a short-tongued generalist that can robber-nectar on tubular flowers; B. hortorum is long-tongued and specializes on deeper blooms. Both nest underground in parks and gardens. Flower-visitor and flower-bobber are the dominant tags per the taxonomy example for bumblebees; the sky-looper secondary movement is retained from the Bombus default to capture inter-patch transit flights.

Bombus bumblebees are among the most important pollinators in Amsterdam, visiting Catalpa, Cercidiphyllum, Cercis, Liriodendron, and Robinia — a suite of large, exotic-flowering trees that attract both short-tongued and long-tongued species. Amsterdam supports a healthy diversity of Bombus species including B. terrestris, B. lapidarius, B. pascuorum, and B. hortorum. Bumblebees are primarily flower-visitors (their most visible and ecologically important behavior) but also make substantial transit flights between flower patches, justifying the sky-looper secondary movement class per the taxonomy examples. The flower-bobber dominant movement captures inter-bloom foraging; sky-looper captures the longer search flights queen and worker bees make between sites.

This grouped entry combines the Bohemian waxwing with two common Amsterdam frugivores — the blackbird (Turdus merula) and mistle thrush — observed on Celtis and Sorbus. Waxwings and mistle thrushes are strictly canopy fruit-feeders, but blackbirds are strongly dual-habitat: they forage extensively on the ground under fruiting trees for worms and fallen berries, making the ground-park secondary habitat and park-roamer secondary movement genuinely accurate for the group. The dominant tags reflect the canopy-feeding waxwing and mistle thrush components; the secondaries capture the blackbird's consistent ground-foraging behavior that distinguishes it from the other two species.

Waxwings (Bombycilla garrulus in Europe, the Bohemian waxwing) are irruptive winter visitors to Amsterdam, arriving in variable numbers from their Scandinavian and Russian breeding grounds when berry crops fail in the north. They are observed on Morus (mulberry) here, stripping the fruit from the canopy in tight flocks. Waxwings are quintessentially tree-canopy birds — they perch, feed, and move between fruiting trees almost exclusively — rarely coming to the ground. The bird category defaults of tree-canopy and tree-flitter are an accurate fit: waxwings move in compact flocks between fruiting trees with direct, starling-like flight, which the tree-flitter animation captures reasonably well.

Bombyx mori, the domestic silkworm, is a fully domesticated insect that cannot survive in the wild; it is incapable of flight and entirely dependent on humans for reproduction. Its association with Morus (white mulberry) in Amsterdam almost certainly reflects a novelty / educational observation rather than a feral population. The larvae feed exclusively on Morus leaves in the canopy; they are entirely immobile beyond slow crawling between leaves. Tree-canopy is the appropriate habitat override (the larvae live and feed on mulberry foliage); idle-only is correct for an animal that does virtually nothing but eat leaf tissue and spin silk. This is a tree-feeding microorganism on Morus in the context of this dataset.

Bradyrhizobium is a genus of nitrogen-fixing soil bacteria that form symbiotic root nodules on leguminous trees, here specifically on Cercis siliquastrum (Judas tree) in Amsterdam. The bacteria live inside root nodules in the soil, fixing atmospheric nitrogen into forms usable by the host tree. They have no independent movement and are invisible to the naked eye — they are effectively sedentary soil microorganisms. Ground-park is the most accurate habitat (they exist in the soil under the tree), and none (static sprite) is the only logical movement class. This is a significant override from the other category defaults (anywhere / idle-only): a soil bacterium has a fixed, underground location.

Bruchidius siliquastri is a bruchid seed weevil that specializes on Cercis siliquastrum (Judas tree), whose seed pods hang decoratively on the tree through summer and autumn. The beetle's larvae develop inside the seeds within the hanging pods in the canopy, consuming the seed contents before pupating inside the pod. Adults emerge and can be found on or near the pods. Tree-canopy is the correct habitat override because the entire life cycle relevant to observation occurs in the hanging seed pods on the tree; idle-only reflects the larval immobility within the seed. This is a tree-feeding microorganism on Cercis, warranting the override from the insect default of flower-visitor.

Wood-boring beetle larvae (jewel beetles and longhorns) tunnel beneath the bark and into the sapwood of weakened or stressed trees, including Celtis street trees in Amsterdam. The larvae spend most of their life cycle as sedentary borers inside the tree, emerging as adults only briefly; in a pixel-art simulation context the bark-boring stage is the dominant visible state. The override shifts them from the insect default of flower-visitor to tree-bark, and movement is idle-only because the burrowing itself is imperceptibly slow. Adults are very short-lived and rarely the focus of observations in Amsterdam.

Pear psyllids are jumping plant-lice that feed exclusively on Pyrus (pear) foliage and shoots, inserting their stylets into leaf tissue and secreting honeydew that attracts sooty mould. In Amsterdam ornamental pear trees (Pyrus calleryana and cultivars) line many streets and they host both Cacopsylla pyri and C. pyricola in summer. The nymphs are entirely sessile on leaves and shoots, while adults jump short distances within the canopy rather than flying any meaningful distance. Override from the insect default to tree-canopy / idle-only as a tree-feeding microorganism on Pyrus.

Callipterinella and Symydobius species are specialist aphids that colonise Betula (birch) catkins, twigs, and undersides of leaves, producing large honeydew deposits that make birches sticky in summer. Amsterdam's many Betula pendula street trees and park trees in Oosterpark and Vondelpark host dense colonies of these aphids from spring through early autumn. They are entirely sedentary within the canopy, overriding the insect default; both genera are tree-feeding microorganisms on Betula, so tree-canopy / idle-only is the correct tag pair.

The horse-chestnut leaf miner is a tiny moth whose larvae create pale blotch mines inside the leaves of Aesculus hippocastanum, the horse-chestnut trees that are extremely common in Amsterdam parks and along canals. By late summer infested trees display brown, papery foliage that appears to have turned early; the mines are visible as pale patches across entire canopies. Larvae are fully sedentary miners inside leaf tissue, so tree-canopy / idle-only is appropriate; override from the insect default of flower-visitor as this is a tree-feeding microorganism on Aesculus.

Roe deer are the smallest native deer of Western Europe and occasional visitors to Amsterdam's larger green spaces, including the Amsterdamse Bos, where they browse on understorey shrubs and low branches of trees such as Pterocarya (Caucasian wingnut). They are active mainly at dawn and dusk and follow meandering paths through park vegetation, matching the mammal default of ground-park / park-roamer precisely. Roe deer do not typically venture into the dense urban core and sightings near city-centre Pterocarya would be exceptional, but the tag is appropriate for the Amsterdamse Bos context.

Goldfinch and Eurasian siskin (Spinus spinus) frequently form mixed feeding flocks on seed-bearing trees in Amsterdam, with Ostrya carpinifolia (hop-hornbeam) providing the seeds that draw this observation. Siskins are specialist canopy feeders on alder and hop-hornbeam catkins, hanging acrobatically from thin branches; goldfinches behave similarly. Both species are small, agile songbirds whose dominant behaviour is short canopy flights between feeding trees, so the default tree-canopy / tree-flitter classification applies without modification.

The European Goldfinch is one of Amsterdam's most colourful year-round residents, nesting in parks and tree-lined streets and feeding on the seeds of Alnus, Liquidambar, Ulmus, and Zelkova — all well represented in the city's street-tree roster. Goldfinches move in small, twittering flocks between tree tops, characteristically clinging to thin seed-bearing branches and flitting short distances from crown to crown. The small-songbird default of tree-canopy / tree-flitter fits perfectly without any override needed.

This row aggregates goldfinch-group and house sparrow species observed opportunistically on Ailanthus altissima (tree of heaven), a pioneer street tree that colonises urban waste ground across Amsterdam. Both Carduelis finches and Passer sparrows are confirmed urban generalists: sparrows forage on pavements and nest in building crevices, while Carduelis finches visit seed-bearing Ailanthus panicles and also perch in canopy. The anywhere habitat best reflects their combined range of placement, and a dual movement of urban-walker (sparrows) and tree-flitter (finches) captures the behavioural split. Override from the bird default because the slot represents multiple species with genuinely mixed site use.

Carulaspis juniper scale insects infest the bark, stems, and foliage of × Cupressocyparis (Leyland cypress), a fast-growing hybrid conifer widely used for hedging in Amsterdam gardens and institutional grounds. Settled crawlers cement themselves to bark with their protective waxy shell and do not move; populations cause yellowing and dieback of affected branchlets. Override from the insect default to tree-bark / idle-only as a tree-feeding microorganism on Cupressocyparis; tags are consistent with the related carulaspis row for Chamaecyparis.

Carulaspis juniperi (juniper scale, family Diaspididae) is an armoured scale insect that anchors to the bark and branchlets of Thuja (arborvitae) and related conifers used in Amsterdam hedges and garden plantings. Once settled, females are permanently immobile beneath their oyster-shaped scale cover; their only mobility phase is the brief crawler stage immediately after hatching. Override from the insect default to tree-bark / idle-only as a tree-feeding microorganism on Thuja; consistent with the other Carulaspis rows in this batch.

Carulaspis scale insects and accompanying tetranychid spider mites colonise the bark and scale-like foliage of Chamaecyparis (Lawson cypress) planted in Amsterdam gardens and urban hedges. Adult scale females are permanently fixed under their waxy shells on bark and branchlets; spider mites spin fine webs on foliage and are for practical purposes stationary at pixel-art scale. Override from the insect default to tree-bark / idle-only as a tree-feeding microorganism on Chamaecyparis.

Cecidophyopsis psilaspis is a microscopic eriophyid mite that infests Taxus (yew) buds, causing them to swell into distinctive round galls — the "big-bud" symptom also known as yew bud mite infestation. Taxus is common in Amsterdam churchyards, formal gardens, and cemetery plantings. Thousands of mites live within a single galled bud; they are entirely sedentary at any scale visible to the naked eye. Override from the arachnid default of anywhere to tree-canopy / idle-only as a gall-forming microorganism on Taxus; consistent with other gall mites in the organism roster.

Cedrobium laportei is a specialist woolly aphid that feeds on the new growth and needles of Cedrus (cedar) trees, producing conspicuous white waxy wool on shoots. Cedrus atlantica, C. libani, and their cultivars are planted as ornamental trees in Amsterdam parks and institutional grounds. The aphids are sedentary colonies on the current-year shoots and do not migrate to an alternate host, remaining on Cedrus throughout the year. Override from the insect default to tree-canopy / idle-only as a tree-feeding microorganism on Cedrus.

The Holly Blue is a dainty metalite-blue butterfly that uses Ilex aquifolium (holly) as its primary spring larval foodplant and ivy flowers as a secondary host; both plants occur widely in Amsterdam parks, gardens, and churchyards. Adults are active flower-visitors that flit between holly and ivy blooms, nectar sources, and damp patches in a characteristic erratic flight close to shrub and tree height. The insect default of flower-visitor / flower-bobber is a natural fit for this species without any override needed.

Cerambycidae (longhorn beetles) are secondary colonisers that bore into the wood of stressed or dying Aesculus (horse-chestnut) trees, a situation increasingly common in Amsterdam where horse-chestnuts are weakened by leaf miner pressure and bleeding canker. Larvae spend one to several years tunnelling through sapwood and heartwood; adults emerge briefly to mate and lay eggs. The dominant life-stage in any tree at any given time is the sedentary larval borer, pinning this entry to tree-bark / idle-only; override from the insect default as a tree-feeding microorganism on Aesculus.

The catalpa sphinx (catalpa hawkmoth) is a North American sphingid moth whose larvae defoliate Catalpa trees; it has been recorded in the Netherlands as an introduced pest on ornamental Catalpa bignonioides, which is planted in Amsterdam parks for its large leaves and showy flowers. Caterpillars cluster on leaves and feed voraciously within the canopy, while the nocturnal adults are seldom visible; the larval stage is the ecologically dominant phase. Override from the insect default to tree-canopy / idle-only as a tree-feeding microorganism on Catalpa.

The Eurasian Treecreeper spirals methodically up tree trunks and large branches, probing bark fissures for invertebrates with its slender decurved bill, then drops to the base of the next tree and repeats. In Amsterdam it is most reliably seen on large mature conifers such as Cedrus and Taxus in Vondelpark and larger cemeteries. Its entire foraging life is spent on bark surfaces, making tree-bark the correct dominant habitat; movement is best described as idle-only in animation terms because the slow spiral creep is below the threshold for a separate movement class. Override from the bird default of tree-canopy / tree-flitter.

This row groups treecreeper (Certhia familiaris) and Eurasian nuthatch (Sitta europaea), both of which forage on the bark of large-diameter trees in Amsterdam parks; Pterocarya (Caucasian wingnut) develops a deeply furrowed bark that is attractive to both species. Nuthatches descend head-first, treecreepers spiral upward, but both spend their active time on bark surfaces. The shared tree-bark habitat is correct; idle-only movement reflects the slow bark-creeping visible in the simulation rather than active flight between trees. Override from the bird default for both constituent species.

Cerura vinula (puss moth) and Furcula species (sallow kitten and related moths) are Notodontidae whose larvae feed on Populus (poplar) and Salix foliage; poplar is abundant along Amsterdam's canals and in the Amsterdamse Bos. The large, vividly marked caterpillars sit exposed on leaves in the canopy and are well-camouflaged when young; adults are nocturnal and seldom observed. The larval stage is the ecologically dominant and visually interesting phase, fixed in the canopy on poplar. Override from the insect default to tree-canopy / idle-only as a tree-feeding microorganism on Populus.

The rose chafer (Cetonia aurata) is a vivid metallic-green scarab beetle that visits open flowers to feed on pollen and petals; it is recorded on Magnolia flowers in Amsterdam parks where Magnolia trees bloom early in spring. Adults fly strongly from flower to flower and are attracted to large, scented blooms; larvae develop in rotting wood or compost elsewhere. The flower-visitor / flower-bobber pairing fits well, but this is an override (not default) because the insect default applies to Hymenoptera/Lepidoptera pollinators rather than flower-feeding beetles — the ecological role is similar but the taxon is not the default case.

Chalcidoid parasitoid wasps — primarily Chrysocharis and Pleurotropis species — are tiny natural enemies of the holly leaf miner (Phytomyza ilicis) and other leaf-mining insects on Ilex and Magnolia in Amsterdam. The adult females walk over leaf surfaces probing mines with their ovipositor to parasitise miner larvae within; their own larvae develop entirely inside the mine. At pixel-art scale they are effectively sessile within the canopy, their foraging movements too small to animate distinctly. Override from the insect default to tree-canopy / idle-only as microorganisms operating within canopy leaf tissue on Ilex and Magnolia.

Chionaspis gleditsiae (and related armoured scale species) infest the bark of Gleditsia triacanthos (honey locust), a popular Amsterdam street tree chosen for its tolerance of urban stress and compacted soils. Adult females are permanently sessile beneath flat, white, elongate scale covers on bark and small branches, drawing sap; infestations cause twig dieback and bark discolouration. Override from the insect default to tree-bark / idle-only as a tree-feeding microorganism on Gleditsia.

This row groups four small songbird species recorded around Cupressocyparis (Leyland cypress) hedges in Amsterdam: greenfinch, dunnock, wren, and robin. All four use dense conifer hedges intensively — greenfinches nest and roost in the foliage, wrens and dunnocks forage in the low scrubby base, robins hold territories in hedge-and-shrub habitats. Tree-canopy is the best available tag for all four since the hedge canopy is the dominant vegetation element; tree-flitter matches the short flights between perches. No override required; the default is the least-wrong single tag for this multi-species row.

Greenfinch and house sparrow are both common Amsterdam residents recorded on Styphnolobium japonicum (Japanese pagoda tree), a street tree that produces seed pods relished by seed-eating birds. Greenfinches are predominantly canopy feeders on seeds; house sparrows forage on the ground but also perch and feed in canopy. In the context of a Styphnolobium observation, both species are in the canopy, and the small-songbird default of tree-canopy / tree-flitter is a reasonable fit for the combined row. No override required.

This row aggregates greenfinch (Chloris chloris) and goldfinch (Carduelis carduelis) observations on Laburnum and Liquidambar street trees in Amsterdam. Both are small to medium finches that forage primarily in the canopy: greenfinches eat seeds from buds and Liquidambar fruits, goldfinches target seed heads. They are resident in Amsterdam year-round and move in small flocks between adjacent trees, matching the small-songbird default of tree-canopy / tree-flitter without modification.

Choristoneura tortricid moths (budworm complex) attack the new shoots and needles of Larix (larch) and occasionally spruce in northern European plantations and ornamental settings; Larix is used in Amsterdam parks and the Amsterdamse Bos. Young larvae mine needles; later instars spin together new growth and feed within the shelter. The caterpillars are sedentary within their web-bound feeding shelter in the canopy and the adult moths are nocturnal. Override from the insect default to tree-canopy / idle-only as a tree-feeding microorganism on Larix.

Chromaphis juglandicola is a specialist aphid that feeds on the undersides of Juglans (walnut) leaflets, forming pale-yellow colonies that excrete heavy honeydew and can cause early leaf-fall. Juglans regia is an occasional tree in Amsterdam parks and larger gardens. The aphid does not use an alternate host and remains on walnut from spring until autumn leaf-fall; colonies are effectively stationary on the leaf underside. Override from the insect default to tree-canopy / idle-only as a tree-feeding microorganism on Juglans.

Chrysomela populi and C. tremula are chunky red-and-black leaf beetles whose adults and larvae feed openly on Populus (poplar) leaves; poplar is common along Amsterdam's canals, in the Amsterdamse Bos, and as street plantings. Larvae congregate on leaves and can skeletonise entire branches; adults overwinter in bark but are prominently visible in the canopy during summer. Despite adults being mobile on the leaf surface, their movement at pixel-art scale is imperceptible. Override from the insect default to tree-canopy / idle-only as a tree-feeding microorganism on Populus.

Cinara cedri is a large, grey-brown conifer aphid that feeds on the shoots and young needles of Cedrus (cedar) trees, producing copious honeydew that coats the ground beneath and supports sooty mould. Cedar trees — Cedrus atlantica, C. libani, and cultivars — are planted in Amsterdam parks such as the Vondelpark and Sarphatipark. Colonies cluster on young shoots and remain on cedar without alternating to another host plant. Override from the insect default to tree-canopy / idle-only as a tree-feeding microorganism on Cedrus; consistent with the related cedrobium-laportei cedar aphid row.

The cypress aphid is a large, grey-brown phloem-feeding aphid that colonises the bark of Chamaecyparis, x Cupressocyparis, and Thuja in Amsterdam parks and garden streets. Colonies cluster tightly on stems and branchlets, barely moving except to probe with stylets, so idle-only is the correct animation. The insect default of flower-visitor / flower-bobber does not apply here; these aphids never visit flowers and spend their entire lives anchored to bark tissue. Tree-bark as the dominant habitat places them correctly on the trunk and branch areas of the host sprite.

The speckled larch aphid is a large lachnid that feeds in dense colonies on the bark of young larch shoots and the main stem of Larix trees. In Amsterdam it is recorded from the single Larix specimen in the dataset and appears in spring and early summer. Colonies are sedentary, covered in a fine wax bloom, and show no foraging movement, making idle-only the correct animation tag. The insect category default of flower-visitor / flower-bobber is overridden because this species is strictly bark-feeding and never associated with flowers.

Cinara pinea and C. pini are among the largest European aphids, forming conspicuous brown colonies on the bark of Pinus shoots and branches. They feed by piercing bark to tap phloem sap and remain anchored in place, occasionally attended by ants. In Amsterdam pines — including those in Vondelpark and the Amsterdamse Bos — colonies persist through the growing season with minimal positional movement, so idle-only is the correct movement class. The insect category default flower-visitor / flower-bobber is overridden in favour of tree-bark / idle-only because these are entirely bark-bound organisms.

The seven-spot ladybird is Amsterdam's most familiar aphid predator, observed on Tilia where it hunts lime aphid colonies in the canopy. Adults and larvae cling to leaves and stems, pausing frequently between aphid catches, so their net positional change is negligible and idle-only captures the on-screen animation well. The insect default of flower-visitor / flower-bobber is overridden because the primary habitat is leafy canopy rather than flowers, and the movement is predatory stalking-in-place rather than flower-bobbing. Tree-canopy placement snaps the sprite to the leaf zone of the nearest Tilia, which matches the field observation context.

This combined entry covers canopy-foraging ladybird beetles and lacewings observed across Juglans, Larix, Magnolia, and Parrotia in Amsterdam. Both groups are aphid and soft-bodied insect predators that spend most of their time perched on leaves or moving slowly between prey clusters; lacewings in particular rest motionless during the day. The insect category default of flower-visitor / flower-bobber is overridden: neither ladybirds nor lacewings are primarily flower-associated in their feeding behaviour. Tree-canopy and idle-only place the sprite realistically in the leafy zone of the host tree.

The hawfinch is a large, powerful finch recorded on Carpinus (hornbeam) in Amsterdam, where it exploits the hard nutlets with its outsized bill. It is a shy, upper-canopy species that moves between trees in short direct flights, fitting the bird category defaults of tree-canopy and tree-flitter well. In Amsterdam it appears mainly during autumn and winter passage, favouring parks and old avenues with mature hornbeam and cherry. The default tags are appropriate without modification.

The larch casebearer moth is a tiny micro-moth whose larva mines individual larch needles from inside a portable silken case, spending the entire larval period on the needles of Larix without ever visiting flowers. In Amsterdam it is associated with the limited Larix stock and is detected mainly through the characteristic needle-mining damage. The insect category default of flower-visitor / flower-bobber is overridden because the organism is a strict foliage miner; tree-canopy and idle-only correctly place the sprite in the needle zone of the host larch and animate it as a near-stationary larval case.

Case-bearer moth caterpillars (family Coleophoridae) recorded on Cornus in Amsterdam are leaf-mining or leaf-fastening larvae that carry a portable case and graze foliage from a fixed position. They are firmly anchored to the host leaf surface and do not travel across the canopy, making idle-only the fitting movement class. The insect default of flower-visitor / flower-bobber is overridden because case-bearers are foliage microhabitat specialists, never flower feeders. Tree-canopy habitat correctly resolves the sprite to the leafy layer of the Cornus host.

This entry covers saproxylic (dead-wood-dependent) beetles associated with Hericium fungus and beech wood decay on Fagus in Amsterdam. Saproxylic beetles spend their larval phase tunnelling inside dead or decaying timber and adult beetles cling to or hide under bark; they are not flower visitors. The insect category default is overridden to tree-bark / idle-only, which correctly places the sprite on the trunk area of the host Fagus and animates it as a nearly immobile bark-surface beetle. Beech with veteran character in the Amsterdamse Bos provides the primary habitat for this guild in the city.

This entry covers the leaf-litter and soil-surface community of springtails and oribatid mites beneath Fagus in Amsterdam. Unlike their bark-dwelling relatives, these organisms live in the decomposing beech leaf litter and humus layer on the ground. Ground-park is the correct habitat, placing the sprite in the park polygon rather than on the tree trunk. The insect category default of flower-visitor / flower-bobber is overridden because this is a litter/soil community; idle-only matches the below-threshold movement of mites and springtails at game sprite scale.

Bark-crevice springtails and psocids (bark lice) recorded on Ginkgo in Amsterdam occupy the fissured bark microhabitat, feeding on algae, lichens, and fungal spores on the bark surface. Psocids are tiny and move slowly and cryptically across bark; springtails in crevices show minimal net displacement at sprite scale. The insect category default of flower-visitor / flower-bobber is overridden because both groups are strictly bark-surface organisms. Tree-bark habitat places the sprite correctly on the trunk area of the host Ginkgo.

Bark-dwelling springtails (Collembola) are tiny hexapods recorded across a diverse range of Amsterdam tree genera including Cercidiphyllum, Metasequoia, Parrotia, Paulownia, Platanus, Populus, and Quercus. They live in bark crevices, feeding on algae, fungi, and decaying organic matter on the bark surface. Their movements are too small and fast to represent as positional sprite movement at the map scale, so idle-only is the practical animation choice. The insect category default of flower-visitor / flower-bobber is overridden because springtails are bark-microhabitat organisms, not flower visitors.

The nut-tree tussock moth (Colocasia coryli) is a noctuid whose caterpillars feed on Carpinus (hornbeam) foliage in Amsterdam parks and avenues. The larva is a foliage-resting caterpillar that moves only slowly between leaves; the adult moth is nocturnal and rests immobile on bark or leaves during the day. Neither the larval nor adult stage involves flower-visiting, so the insect category default of flower-visitor / flower-bobber is overridden in favour of tree-canopy / idle-only, which places the sprite in the foliage zone of the host hornbeam and renders it as a near-stationary caterpillar or resting moth.

This combined entry records wood pigeons and corvids (jackdaws, crows, or magpies) together on Metasequoia and Nyssa in Amsterdam. Wood pigeons roost and forage in the canopy while corvids — especially hooded crows and jackdaws — regularly descend to paved areas to forage. The dual habitat of tree-canopy and anywhere reflects this genuine split, and the dual movement of tree-flitter and urban-walker captures the between-tree flight of pigeons alongside the pavement-walking of corvids. The bird default of tree-canopy / tree-flitter is extended rather than replaced to account for the corvid ground-foraging component of this mixed observation.

This three-species observation on Ginkgo in Amsterdam spans an urban generalist mix: wood pigeon (a canopy roosting bird), Eurasian magpie (equally at home in trees and on pavements), and hooded crow (a ground-foraging corvid). Because the three species span such distinct ecological roles, anywhere is the most honest dominant habitat rather than forcing tree-canopy. The dual movement of tree-flitter and urban-walker captures the between-tree movement common to all three and the pavement-foraging typical of the two corvids. The bird category default of tree-canopy / tree-flitter is overridden to reflect the strong corvid ground element in this mixed group.

Wood pigeons and collared doves are both canopy-roosting and canopy-nesting species frequently observed together in the dense evergreen foliage of x Cupressocyparis (Leyland cypress) in Amsterdam. Both species spend long periods sitting motionless in the canopy, making idle-only the dominant animation, with tree-flitter as a secondary for short movements between adjacent trees. Tree-canopy is the correct habitat; the bird category default movement of tree-flitter is demoted to secondary because the defining observation context for this combination is roosting in dense cypress foliage, not active flitting.

This combined entry covers wood pigeons and roosting blackbirds and other passerines observed in the dense canopies of Cercidiphyllum, Chamaecyparis, Cornus, Ilex, Liquidambar, Taxodium, Thuja, and Zelkova across Amsterdam. The common observation context is roosting birds settled in dense foliage, making idle-only the dominant movement animation; tree-flitter is the secondary for when individuals move between adjacent trees. Tree-canopy is the correct habitat for all species in this group since the primary observation is roosting in the leafy layer, not ground-foraging. The default tree-flitter movement is demoted to secondary because the roosting focus makes idle-only the dominant animation.

The carrion crow (Corvus corone) and its sibling taxon the hooded crow (Corvus cornix) are the dominant large corvids of Amsterdam, recorded here on Juglans (walnut). These birds exploit almost every urban habitat: they roost in tree canopies, forage on pavements, lawns, canal banks, and rubbish, and cache food on rooftops. Anywhere is the correct habitat class, and the dual movement of tree-flitter and urban-walker captures the between-tree hops and the deliberate pavement stalking that characterise daily crow behaviour. The bird category default of tree-canopy / tree-flitter is overridden because the ground-foraging component is too significant to omit.

Rooks (Corvus frugilegus) and carrion crows together in Platanus (plane tree) avenues are a characteristic winter sight along Amsterdam's canal streets and in parks like the Vondelpark. Rooks nest colonially in tall trees and commute to short grassland and pavements to probe for invertebrates, while carrion crows are year-round urban generalists. Anywhere correctly covers both species' promiscuous habitat use; tree-flitter and urban-walker together capture the tree-roosting and pavement-foraging that alternate throughout the day. The bird category default is overridden because the ground-foraging component is essential to both species' ecology in the city.

This entry captures a crow and magpie roosting assembly recorded in Gleditsia (honey locust) in Amsterdam. Crows (Corvus corone / C. cornix) and Eurasian magpies (Pica pica) are Amsterdam's archetypal urban corvids, equally at home perching in tree canopies and foraging on pavements, lawns, and canal banks. Anywhere is the correct habitat because neither species has a single dominant microhabitat. The dual movement of tree-flitter and urban-walker reflects the roost-to-pavement commute these birds make daily; the bird category default of tree-canopy / tree-flitter is overridden to include the ground-foraging component that corvids demonstrably express.

The sycamore lace bug is a North American tingid invasive now widespread in continental Europe and well established on London plane (Platanus) in Amsterdam. Adults and nymphs feed gregariously on the undersides of Platanus leaves, causing characteristic pale stippling; they remain on the leaf surface for extended periods and only move slowly between feeding sites. The insect category default of flower-visitor / flower-bobber is overridden to tree-canopy / idle-only because this species is strictly a foliage undersurface feeder on a single host genus, with no association with flowers.

The white-spotted pinion is a noctuid moth whose caterpillar feeds inside the developing buds and leaves of elm (Ulmus), an increasingly rare host in Amsterdam following Dutch elm disease but still present in some parks and canal streets. The larva mines buds in spring and the adult rests cryptically on bark or among leaves during the day, making idle-only the correct animation for any daylight sprite. The insect category default of flower-visitor / flower-bobber is overridden to tree-canopy / idle-only because the species is wholly dependent on elm foliage and has no flower-visiting stage relevant to map animation.

This combined entry covers the goat moth (Cossus cossus) and clearwing moths (Synanthedon spp.) whose larvae bore through the wood and bark of Castanea and Salix in Amsterdam. Both species spend several larval years tunnelling inside the trunk, and adults rest briefly on bark surfaces after emergence before dispersing. The insect category default of flower-visitor / flower-bobber is overridden in favour of tree-bark / idle-only because the larval stage — the ecologically dominant phase — is entirely within the bark and wood, and the adult stage on-tree is also a resting posture. The goat moth is notable for the pungent goat-like odour of its larvae, which can sometimes be detected at infested willows along Amsterdam canals.

This entry covers the cryptogamic crust of foliose lichens and cushion mosses colonising the bark of Ginkgo in Amsterdam. Ginkgo's rough, deeply fissured bark provides an excellent substrate for both groups, and the clean-air-sensitive lichen community in Amsterdam is increasingly diverse as air quality improves. The lichen category defaults of tree-bark and none fit perfectly: cryptogams are sessile organisms permanently adhered to the bark surface with no movement at any life stage. No override is needed.

The chestnut weevil (Curculio elephas) is a long-snouted curculionid whose larvae develop inside the nuts of Castanea (sweet chestnut). The female drills into developing chestnuts using her elongated rostrum and lays eggs inside; the larvae feed within the nut and emerge in autumn to pupate in soil. Adult weevils cling to foliage and nuts in the canopy and move very slowly. The insect category default of flower-visitor / flower-bobber is overridden to tree-canopy / idle-only because all activity is centred on the nut-bearing canopy, not on flowers, and the adults' positional movement is negligible at sprite scale.

Acorn weevils (Curculio glandium and the related C. elephas on oak) are classic canopy-foraging curculionids whose females use their very long curved rostrum to bore into developing acorns on Quercus to deposit eggs. Amsterdam's numerous oak-lined streets and park oaks support a resident population, most visible in late summer when adults are active on foliage and acorns. The insect category default of flower-visitor / flower-bobber is overridden to tree-canopy / idle-only because the entire life cycle revolves around acorns in the canopy, and adults move only slowly across leaf and twig surfaces.

The nut weevil (Curculio nucum) is a hazel-specialist curculionid whose larvae develop inside hazelnuts (Corylus). The female drills through the nut shell with her long rostrum to lay a single egg per nut; larvae then consume the kernel. Adult beetles feed on hazel leaves and buds in the canopy during spring and are sluggish walkers, spending most of their time stationary. The insect category default of flower-visitor / flower-bobber is overridden to tree-canopy / idle-only because the ecological focus is entirely on hazel nuts and foliage, and the adult's on-tree behaviour is characteristically immobile.

This entry covers the diverse community of over 100 weevil species (superfamily Curculionoidea) associated with willows (Salix) in the wider Amsterdam region, including canal-bank pollard willows and park specimens. Willow weevils include leaf-rollers, gall-formers, and bark-miners; all life stages are confined to the willow host, either on leaves and shoots in the canopy or in stems and bark. The insect category default of flower-visitor / flower-bobber is overridden to tree-canopy / idle-only because this guild is comprised of leaf and tissue feeders, not pollinators, and adults on willow foliage barely move at map sprite scale.

This entry captures Blue Tit and Great Tit observations specifically associated with hazel (Corylus) trees in Amsterdam. Both species exploit hazel catkins for seeds in late winter and systematically probe hazel twigs for aphids and moth larvae in summer. Their behaviour is identical to the main slug: rapid canopy foraging with frequent hops between branches and nearby trees. The tree-canopy habitat and tree-flitter movement defaults apply without modification. Being observed on a single Corylus genus makes no difference to how they are animated or placed.

Blue Tits and Great Tits are among the most abundant and visible birds in Amsterdam's parks and street trees. They forage actively through the canopy of deciduous trees — especially oaks, maples, lindens, and elms — picking insects and invertebrates from leaf surfaces and crevices. Both species hop rapidly between branches and flit to neighbouring trees every few seconds, making them ideal candidates for the tree-flitter animation. In Amsterdam they are present year-round and are strongly associated with the 17 genera listed in observations. The default bird tags fit perfectly without any override needed.

A broader family-level grouping (Paridae) recorded on Liriodendron, Magnolia, Malus and Ostrya in Amsterdam. Blue Tit and Great Tit are the dominant members of this family in the Netherlands and their canopy habits are consistent across all four host genera. They glean insects from foliage and buds, flitting rapidly from branch to branch and tree to tree throughout the year. The default bird tags — tree-canopy habitat and tree-flitter movement — are accurate and no override is needed for this paridae aggregate slug.

Blue tit (Cyanistes caeruleus) and great tit (Parus major) are Amsterdam's most abundant and visible small songbirds, recorded across a wide range of host trees including Aesculus, Amelanchier, Carpinus, Ilex, Robinia, and Styphnolobium. Both species are quintessential canopy foragers that move restlessly between trees in short arcing flights, perfectly matching the bird category defaults of tree-canopy and tree-flitter. They are year-round residents found in virtually every Amsterdam park, garden, and tree-lined street. No override is needed; the default tags describe this pair exactly.

Cydia illutana and related larch cone moths spend their larval stages inside larch cones and shoot tips, making them almost entirely canopy-bound on Larix trees in Amsterdam. Adults are small nocturnal tortricid moths that rest motionless on bark or foliage during the day. Because they spend their lives either concealed inside plant tissue or resting still, flower-visitor and flower-bobber defaults are completely wrong; tree-canopy with idle-only captures their actual presence on the tree without the motion they simply do not perform. They are an override from the insect default.

The codling moth is the classic apple and pear fruit borer, with larvae tunnelling inside the developing fruit of Juglans, Malus and Pyrus. In Amsterdam's urban orchards and park trees these moths are present throughout summer, but adults are cryptic night-fliers that rest immobile on bark or foliage during daylight. The larval stage is hidden entirely within fruit tissue and the adult rarely moves when at rest. Tree-canopy placement is correct since the lifecycle is centred on the fruit-bearing canopy; idle-only replaces the inappropriate flower-bobber default because there is no meaningful positional movement to animate.

Cydia splendana is a tortricid moth whose larvae bore into developing sweet chestnut nuts (Castanea) to feed on the kernel. In Amsterdam this species is observed on the relatively rare Castanea trees in parks and avenues. Adults are small, subtly patterned moths that rest immobile on bark or foliage and are almost never seen in active flight during daylight. The canopy of the host chestnut is the correct placement zone, and idle-only replaces the insect default of flower-bobber since these moths are sedentary during any visible portion of their lifecycle.

Oak gall wasps (Cynipidae) are a diverse family of tiny parasitoid wasps whose larvae develop entirely inside galls they induce on Quercus leaves, buds, catkins, acorns or roots. Amsterdam's oak trees host dozens of cynipid species producing the characteristic spherical, spangle, cherry and artichoke galls visible in parks and along streets. Adults are minute, dark wasps that are rarely visible in flight; the observable manifestation of their presence is always the stationary gall structure anchored in the canopy. Tree-canopy and idle-only therefore replace the insect defaults, as no flower-bobbing behaviour is relevant.

Dasineura pyri is a tiny gall midge whose larvae cause the characteristic inward rolling of pear leaf margins on Pyrus trees. The insect spends its larval and pupal stages entirely hidden within the curled leaf tissue in the canopy. Adult midges are extremely small and fragile, emerging briefly in spring to mate and oviposit but otherwise imperceptible during any visible window. Tree-canopy is the correct placement since all observable signs — the rolled, discoloured leaves — are in the canopy; idle-only replaces flower-bobber as there is no animated movement meaningful at sprite scale.

This combined slug groups the Great Spotted Woodpecker with the Eurasian Nuthatch, both recorded on the beech (Fagus) tree in Amsterdam. The Nuthatch is a compact bird that runs head-first down trunks and along major branches probing bark crevices for insects and cached nuts — a lifestyle almost identical in placement terms to the woodpecker. Both species are bark specialists who also spend time in the canopy and make short flights between nearby trees. Tree-bark is the dominant habitat with tree-canopy as secondary; tree-flitter is the primary movement with idle-only secondary for the frequent stationary foraging postures of both species.

The Great Spotted Woodpecker is a striking black-and-white bird resident in Amsterdam's larger parks and along Betula and Cedrus avenues. It spends the great majority of its foraging time clinging to trunks and major branches, drilling for beetle larvae and extracting seeds from cones, making tree-bark the dominant habitat over the canopy default. Between foraging bouts it flies to a neighbouring tree with an undulating flight, so tree-flitter is retained as primary movement, while the long stationary drilling sessions warrant idle-only as a secondary. This pair of habitat and movement overrides distinguishes it from ordinary canopy songbirds.

This slug represents the same guild of armoured scales and mealybugs observed on Nyssa trees in Amsterdam. Nyssa (tupelo) is an uncommon ornamental in the city and the scales found on it behave identically to those on other genera: they are bark-anchored, sedentary sap-feeders that never move at a scale meaningful for animation. Tree-bark placement and idle-only movement are the appropriate overrides from the insect defaults of flower-visitor and flower-bobber, consistent with the diaspididae slug above.

Armoured scales (Diaspididae) and mealybugs (Pseudococcidae) are sap-sucking hemipterans that attach to bark, twigs and sometimes leaves of Celtis and Cercidiphyllum trees in Amsterdam. Armoured scales in particular are permanently sedentary once settled, secreting a waxy protective cover and spending their entire feeding life fixed to the bark surface. Mealybugs move very slowly when young but are functionally stationary at sprite scale. Tree-bark is the correct dominant habitat — not flower-visitor — and idle-only replaces flower-bobber entirely, as these insects perform no meaningful locomotion.

Pine sawflies are hymenopteran larvae that feed gregariously on pine needles (Pinus) in tight clusters, often stripping entire branches in Amsterdam's park pines. The larvae are the most conspicuous life stage — bright green or yellowish caterpillar-like grubs sitting motionless in rows on needle bundles. Adult sawflies are short-lived, unobtrusive and rarely visible. The entire observable lifecycle occurs within the tree canopy on needles and shoots; idle-only captures the gregarious sedentary larval clusters, replacing the insect defaults of flower-visitor and flower-bobber which are entirely inappropriate.

This aggregate slug covers the diverse community of flies, bees, wasps, beetles and other insects observed visiting hawthorn (Crataegus) blossom in Amsterdam. Crataegus is a major urban flowering shrub and small tree whose heavy white-scented blooms in May attract a wide range of pollinating insects. The assemblage — hoverflies, solitary bees, bumblebees, beetles — all exhibit classic flower-visitor behaviour, moving from bloom to bloom within and between trees. The insect defaults of flower-visitor habitat and flower-bobber movement fit this group perfectly and no override is required.

The lesser stag beetle is a saproxylic beetle whose larvae develop inside the rotting heartwood of dead or dying ash (Fraxinus) stumps and roots in Amsterdam's parks. Adults are chunky, black beetles that are most often found clinging motionless to dead wood or stumps at ground level and on lower trunks. Tree-bark is the dominant habitat because the beetle is tightly associated with decaying wood surfaces, with ground-park as a secondary since adults are frequently found on the soil surface near decaying roots. Idle-only replaces flower-bobber as these beetles are essentially sedentary at the scale relevant to the animation system.

The sycamore aphid is one of the most common and visible aphids on Acer (maple) trees throughout Amsterdam, forming dense colonies on the undersides of leaves from spring through autumn. Like all aphids it is essentially stationary once feeding — individuals sit immobile piercing leaf veins and exuding honeydew that drips onto pavements below. Tree-canopy is the correct placement since colonies occupy the leaf surfaces; idle-only replaces the insect default of flower-bobber because the aphid performs no flower-visiting or meaningful locomotion at sprite scale.

The Oriental chestnut gall wasp is an invasive cynipid from East Asia now established in parts of the Netherlands, including Amsterdam where it attacks sweet chestnut (Castanea). It induces distinctive green-and-red galls on buds, leaves and catkins in the canopy, where the larva develops entirely concealed inside the gall tissue. Adults emerge in summer as tiny, inconspicuous wasps that mate and oviposit rapidly before dying; the visible manifestation is always the stationary gall in the canopy. Tree-canopy placement and idle-only movement override the irrelevant insect defaults.

Dysaphis plantaginea (rosy apple aphid) and Aphis pomi (green apple aphid) are two common aphid species that colonise apple (Malus) shoots and leaves in Amsterdam's parks and urban gardens. Rosy apple aphid causes characteristic leaf curling and distortion while green apple aphid forms dense colonies on young shoots; both species are sedentary feeders that remain stationary on the plant tissue. Tree-canopy captures their presence in the leafy zone of the host apple tree, and idle-only replaces the default flower-bobber since neither species visits flowers or moves meaningfully at animation scale.

Dysaphis pyri and its close relative D. plantaginea are pear-specialising aphids observed on Pyrus trees in Amsterdam. They colonise leaf undersides and shoot tips from spring, causing the leaves to curl and redden in characteristic clusters that make colonies easy to spot in the canopy. These aphids are sedentary feeders — once settled on a feeding site they remain essentially stationary, piercing the phloem and extracting sap. Tree-canopy and idle-only are the correct overrides from the insect defaults, consistent with all other aphid slugs in this batch.

This aggregate covers aphid species — primarily Dysaphis sorbi and woolly aphids (Eriosoma) — recorded on rowan and whitebeam (Sorbus) trees in Amsterdam. Dysaphis sorbi colonises leaf undersides producing leaf curls, while Eriosoma species create the distinctive fluffy white woolly masses on bark and shoots. Both are sedentary feeders anchored to the plant surface; woolly aphids can occupy either bark or canopy zones but canopy is dominant for the observable leaf-curling species. Tree-canopy and idle-only override the insect defaults, as with all other aphid slugs in this batch.

The birch shieldbug is a medium-sized green and brown shieldbug strongly associated with birch (Betula) trees, where adults and nymphs feed on developing catkins and seeds in the canopy. In Amsterdam it is found in parks with mature birch trees, typically sitting immobile on leaves or bark during the day. Although adults can fly, they are rarely seen in sustained flight and spend most of their visible time resting or slowly feeding. Tree-canopy is the correct dominant habitat over the insect default flower-visitor, and idle-only replaces flower-bobber as the shieldbug's typical demeanour is thoroughly sedentary.

Elatobium abietinum (green spruce aphid) and Cinara pilicornis (large spruce aphid) are two major aphid pests of Norway spruce (Picea) found in Amsterdam's parks and avenues. Elatobium colonises the older needles of lower branches in dense, inconspicuous colonies that cause yellowing and premature needle drop; Cinara forms larger visible clusters on young shoots. Both are sedentary needle-feeders that move negligibly at sprite scale. Tree-canopy (the needle zones of the spruce crown) and idle-only are the correct overrides from the insect flower-visitor defaults.

Enarmonia formosana (cherry bark tortrix) larvae bore into the bark and cambium of Prunus trees, creating characteristic gummy entry wounds on trunks and main branches. Argyresthia pruniella larvae mine cherry fruitlets. Both moths rest motionless on bark surfaces during the day. The dominant habitat for this pairing is tree-bark rather than canopy because the most diagnostic and damaging life stage — the bark-boring larva — is anchored to the trunk surface. Idle-only replaces flower-bobber; adults are nocturnal and the observable bark-damage signs are completely stationary.

This aggregate covers moths in Geometridae and Notodontidae — including the spectacular Kentish glory (Endromis versicolora) — whose larvae feed on birch (Betula) foliage in Amsterdam. Geometrid larvae are the classic "inchworm" caterpillars that rest on twigs mimicking small twigs; notodontid larvae also rest on foliage with characteristic postures. Adults of both families are large, cryptically patterned moths that rest motionless on bark or foliage during daylight. Tree-canopy is the dominant habitat for the leaf-feeding larval stage, and idle-only captures the characteristic still-resting behaviour of both larvae and adults.

Eriophyes tiliae is the mite responsible for the distinctive nail-like or pouch-like galls (Aceria-type ceratonea or unguicula forms) that project from the upper surface of linden (Tilia) leaves throughout Amsterdam. These mites are invisible to the naked eye and spend their entire lives within the gall tissue or on leaf surfaces. The visible feature — the red or green nail galls dotting the leaf — is firmly within the canopy of Tilia street trees that line most of Amsterdam's boulevards. Tree-canopy overrides the arachnid default of anywhere, while idle-only is retained as movement.

Eriophyes gall mites are microscopic arachnids observed on hawthorn (Crataegus) in Amsterdam, where they induce various leaf-surface galls and erinea (felt-like patches of abnormal leaf hairs). These mites are far too small to see individually; their presence is indicated by the visible gall structures on leaves in the canopy.
