The arachnid default of anywhere and idle-only is partially correct in movement but wrong in habitat — tree-canopy is far more specific and accurate for these strictly leaf-surface mites. Habitat is overridden from anywhere to tree-canopy; movement idle-only is retained from the default.

The lime aphid is among the most abundant and noticeable aphids in Amsterdam, forming dense colonies on the undersides of linden (Tilia) leaves from late spring through summer. It produces copious honeydew that falls as sticky rain on cars and pavements beneath linden avenues — a well-known nuisance in the city. Colonies are stationary; individual aphids pierce the leaf phloem and feed without moving for extended periods. Tree-canopy is the exact correct placement for this canopy leaf-feeder, and idle-only replaces the insect default flower-bobber as no pollinating or flower-visiting behaviour occurs.

Euceraphis betulae is a large, elegant aphid found on silver birch (Betula) trees in Amsterdam's parks and along birch-lined paths. Unlike many aphids it tends to occur in scattered individuals rather than dense colonies, but it is still a sedentary canopy feeder that sits immobile on leaf undersides extracting sap. Adults are winged but flights are brief dispersal events rather than sustained visible movement. Tree-canopy captures the leaf-surface foraging zone correctly, and idle-only replaces the inappropriate insect defaults of flower-visitor and flower-bobber.

Fascista cercerisella is a small gracillariid moth whose larvae fold and tie the leaves of Cercis (Judas tree) in Amsterdam. The aggregate also covers the broader community of some 10–19 Lepidoptera larval species associated with this ornamental tree. All these larvae feed and shelter within folded or mined leaf tissue in the canopy, and adults are inconspicuous micro-moths that rest motionless on foliage or bark. Tree-canopy is the correct habitat for this leaf-feeding guild; idle-only replaces flower-bobber, as none of these species exhibit flower-visiting behaviour as their dominant trait.

The Purple hairstreak is an oak-specialist butterfly whose adults spend nearly their entire lives in the canopy of Quercus trees, rarely descending to ground level. In Amsterdam they are tied to street-side and park oaks, where they feed on honeydew secreted by aphids rather than seeking flowers. Their flight is a short fluttering movement among the upper branches, making flower-bobber the closest animation analogue despite the non-flower food source. The override from the insect default reflects their strict canopy residency and lack of interest in ground-level flowers.

Fomitopsis betulina (formerly Piptoporus betulinus) is a bracket fungus exclusive to birch trees, forming large kidney-shaped fruiting bodies on the bark of Betula trunks in Amsterdam parks and cemeteries. The Mycetophilidae fungus gnats listed here breed inside the decaying bracket, living out their larval stages within the sporocarp tissue — effectively co-located with the fungus on bark. As a fungal organism the category default of tree-bark / none applies cleanly, with the gnat association considered incidental to placement. The bracket is strictly non-motile and the static sprite is appropriate.

The common earwig is a nocturnal generalist that shelters under loose bark on urban trees such as Parrotia, Paulownia and Pyrus, then forages at night on the ground among leaf litter and low vegetation in Amsterdam parks and gardens. Its movement is below the animation threshold — earwigs creep very slowly and shelter motionlessly during daylight hours, fitting idle-only. The override from the insect default (flower-visitor / flower-bobber) is warranted because earwigs are bark-sheltering detritivores rather than flower pollinators, with a secondary ground-park tag reflecting night-time foraging.

Red wood ants build large mound nests at the base of Pinus trees and forage extensively over the ground in parks and woodland patches in and around Amsterdam. While they do ascend pine trunks and canopy to tend aphids and harvest resin, their dominant placement is the ground-park mound and foraging trails, making ground-park the primary habitat. The park-roamer animation captures their constant ground movement, with idle-only as secondary for workers motionless on the mound surface. The override reflects their ground-dominant ecology, distinct from canopy-tending ants.

This entry covers wood ants and sugar-tending Formicidae observed on Acer trees in Amsterdam, primarily tending aphid colonies in the canopy for honeydew. Workers travel from ground nests up into the canopy and back, so both tree-canopy (dominant — where they are visible tending aphids) and ground-park (where nests and foraging trails are found) apply. Their movement on the tree surface is a slow crawl below the animation threshold, making idle-only appropriate. The override from the insect default reflects their canopy-tending ecology rather than flower-visiting.

This entry captures ants (exemplified by Lasius niger) observed specifically in the canopy of Liriodendron tulip trees, tending aphid colonies and harvesting honeydew. Their placement is tree-canopy because the observational context is on-tree honeydew feeding rather than ground nesting. On the trunk and branch surfaces they move slowly enough to be rendered as idle-only. The override from the insect default reflects a tree-canopy microhabitat association rather than the flower-visitor default for insects.

Frankia alni is an actinobacterium that forms nitrogen-fixing root nodules on Alnus (alder) trees, which line many Amsterdam canals and parks. The organism is entirely endosymbiotic within root tissue — invisible above ground but inseparable from the host tree's root zone. Placement at the tree coordinate with tree-rooted is the most accurate representation available in the vocabulary, though the organism technically lives below ground. Movement is none since bacteria are rendered as static sprites. The override from the other default (anywhere / idle-only) pins it correctly to its host tree.

Chaffinch and Brambling are common finches in Amsterdam's beech-dominated parks, observed feeding in Fagus canopy and descending in flocks to forage on fallen beech mast on the ground below. The dual habitat reflects this well-documented alternation: canopy for singing, roosting and invertebrate gleaning, ground-park for seed foraging under the trees in autumn and winter. Accordingly tree-flitter is dominant but park-roamer captures the ground-feeding flocks. The override is warranted because these species demonstrably split time between canopy and ground more than a default small passerine would.

This entry groups chaffinch, goldfinch, greenfinch, siskin and various Sylvia warblers commonly observed in Chamaecyparis conifers in Amsterdam parks and cemeteries. All are classic small songbirds that forage and shelter within tree canopies and hop briskly between branches and neighbouring trees. The default bird tags of tree-canopy and tree-flitter fit this diverse grouping exactly. These species form the core of the bird default category in the taxonomy.

The Eurasian Jay is one of Amsterdam's most characteristic park birds, strongly associated with Quercus, Aesculus, Juglans and Pterocarya — all mast-producing trees from which it harvests and caches nuts. Jays are conspicuous in the canopy during foraging bouts but spend substantial time on the ground in parks searching for cached acorns or gleaning fallen nuts, making a dual habitat tag essential. Tree-flitter captures their bold canopy hops between adjacent trees, while park-roamer reflects their deliberate ground walks between caching sites. The override from the bird default is necessary given the jay's distinctly bimodal canopy-ground lifestyle.

This entry covers geometrid and noctuid moth caterpillars that feed on Acer, Picea, Tilia and Zelkova foliage in Amsterdam street trees and parks. As larvae they are sedentary leaf-feeders clinging to foliage, making tree-canopy and idle-only the accurate tags. Adult moths are nocturnal and seldom visible during daytime simulation hours, so the caterpillar life stage dominates the placement logic. The override from the insect default (flower-visitor / flower-bobber) reflects their strict leaf-eating caterpillar ecology and lack of flower-visiting behaviour.

Spruce sawflies (Gilpinia hercyniae and relatives) are specialist defoliators of Picea, with larvae feeding gregariously on spruce needles in the canopy. In Amsterdam, isolated ornamental Picea in parks and gardens can host these sawflies, though outbreaks are rare in urban settings. The larvae are essentially sedentary on foliage and move only to adjacent needle clusters, making idle-only the correct movement tag. The override from the insect default places them in tree-canopy rather than as flower-visitors since they are strict conifer foliage specialists.

The mulberry leaf-webber is a crambid moth whose larvae spin silk webs around Morus foliage, feeding within the webbed nest in the canopy. Amsterdam has a small but growing population of Morus trees as ornamentals and this specialist moth has been noted on them. Larvae are entirely canopy-resident and sedentary within their web shelters, making idle-only the correct movement. The override from the insect default is warranted: this is a leaf-webbing tree specialist, not a flower-visitor.

Gracillariid and nepticulid leaf-mining moths have larvae that tunnel between the upper and lower epidermis of Ostrya leaves, leaving characteristic blotch or linear mines visible to observers. Ostrya carpinifolia (hop-hornbeam) is a relatively rare street tree in Amsterdam but does appear in specialist plantings where these micromoths follow. The mining larvae are entirely immobile within the leaf tissue, making idle-only definitive. The override from the insect default correctly anchors them to tree-canopy foliage rather than flowers.

The beech blight aphid forms large, densely packed colonies on the bark of Taxodium and beech trunks, preferring the lower trunk and major branches rather than the leafy canopy tips. Their conspicuous white woolly wax and clustering behaviour make them highly visible on bark surfaces. In Amsterdam, Taxodium distichum appears in park and canal-edge plantings where this aphid can establish. Because colonies sit on the bark surface rather than within foliage, tree-bark is more accurate than tree-canopy, distinguishing them from leaf-feeding species. They are sessile and idle-only.

The aspen hoverfly is a Red Data Book hoverfly whose larvae develop in wet decaying wood of Populus (aspen and related poplars), particularly in sap-runs beneath the bark. Adults emerge to visit flowers for pollen and nectar, making them a dual-habitat organism: the larval stage is tree-bark anchored while adults are flower-visitors. In Amsterdam they are associated with older Populus trees in parks with some decay. Tree-bark is dominant because the breeding microhabitat drives placement, with flower-visitor secondary for adult feeding. Accordingly idle-only (larvae in bark) dominates, with flower-bobber for adults.

Heterarthrus and Fenusa are leaf-mining sawflies whose larvae feed between the leaf layers of Betula, creating blotch mines that turn leaves brown by late summer. Birch trees are among the most common urban trees in Amsterdam and these sawflies are widespread on them. As with all leaf-miners, the larvae are sessile within the mine and idle-only is the correct movement class. The override from the insect default anchors them correctly to tree-canopy rather than the flower-visitor default.

This entry captures the assemblage of honeybees and various flies observed feeding on aphid honeydew secreted onto Juglans foliage, rather than visiting flowers directly. In Amsterdam walnut trees in parks produce sticky honeydew-coated leaves that attract Apis mellifera and calyptrate flies in large numbers. The habitat is firmly tree-canopy (honeydew is collected on foliage and branches) rather than flower-visitor despite honeybees being classic pollinators. Flower-bobber is the closest movement analogue as the insects flit between sticky leaf clusters in a similar pattern to flower-to-flower visits. The override reflects the atypical non-floral food source driving their canopy placement.

Liquidambar styraciflua (sweetgum) produces extrafloral nectaries on its stems and petioles that attract honeybees and hoverflies seeking sugar outside of the flowering period. This Amsterdam street tree is increasingly planted as a climate-adapted species and its EFNs are actively exploited by pollinators visiting the canopy zone. The habitat is tree-canopy because nectary secretions occur on foliage and young stems rather than flowers, but flower-bobber movement fits the short flit-and-feed pattern these insects show between EFN sites. The override distinguishes this non-flower canopy foraging from the standard flower-visitor default.

Hylesinus and Leperisinus fraxini are bark beetles that bore into the bark and cambium of Fraxinus ash trees to breed, creating distinctive D-shaped exit holes and gallery systems. Amsterdam has extensive street plantings of Fraxinus excelsior and F. pennsylvanica, and these bark beetles (along with the ash dieback pathogen Hymenoscyphus fraxineus) are significant threats to the urban ash stock. Adults are short-lived and rarely seen in flight; placement on tree bark is definitive and movement is idle-only for the sedentary bark-boring life stage. The override from insect default is necessary given their strict bark-dwelling ecology.

Illinoia liriodendri is an aphid specialist on Liriodendron tulipifera (tulip tree) and Magnolia, feeding on the undersides of leaves in the canopy and excreting copious honeydew. Both Liriodendron and Magnolia appear in Amsterdam parks and as ornamental street trees, making this aphid a genuine urban Amsterdam species. Like all sedentary phloem-feeding aphids, it is idle-only in movement terms — colonies sit motionless on leaf undersides. The override from the insect default correctly places it in tree-canopy as a leaf-feeding specialist rather than a flower-visitor.

Ips typographus is the most destructive spruce bark beetle in Europe, boring through the outer bark of Picea to lay eggs and construct breeding galleries in the phloem. In Amsterdam, ornamental Picea trees in parks can be colonised particularly when weakened by drought, and the beetle has expanded its range under climate warming. The organism lives its entire productive life on and beneath the bark surface — tree-bark is the definitive habitat. Movement is idle-only as the beetle is visible only as an exit-hole cluster on bark; the boring activity is below the animation threshold. The override is necessary given the bark-dwelling lifestyle versus the insect flower-visitor default.

Jadera haematoloma is a seed-feeding rhopalid bug originally from North America that has become established in Europe wherever its host Koelreuteria paniculata (goldenrain tree) is planted. In Amsterdam, Koelreuteria is used as an ornamental street and park tree and Jadera aggregates in large numbers on fallen seed pods beneath the trees, feeding on seeds at ground level. Ground-park is the dominant habitat because the bugs feed on fallen seeds under the tree rather than in the canopy. They are sedentary seed-suckers and idle-only in movement terms. The override moves them from the insect flower-visitor default to reflect their ground seed-feeding ecology.

The poplar hawk moth is a large sphingid moth whose larvae feed exclusively on Populus and Salix foliage in the canopy, resting motionless during the day on branches or leaves with their cryptic bark-grey wings. Adults are crepuscular and nocturnal, flying over tree canopies in long looping paths to locate mates and host trees. Tree-canopy is the dominant habitat for both larval feeding and adult daytime roosting, making it the correct anchor. Idle-only is dominant (capturing the motionless daytime resting posture that would be the primary visible state), with sky-looper secondary for nocturnal adult flight. The override from the insect default is necessary as this is a tree-canopy specialist rather than a flower-visitor.

Halictid sweat bees are small ground-nesting solitary bees observed visiting Gleditsia (honey locust) flowers in Amsterdam, a tree common along streets and in parks. While adults visit flowers for pollen and nectar — fitting the flower-visitor default — females also nest in bare or sparsely vegetated ground in parks, requiring a secondary ground-park tag. Flower-bobber is the dominant movement for foraging adults, with park-roamer secondary for ground-level nest-searching and nest approach behaviour. The override adds the ground-park secondary to the otherwise default-fitting insect tags.

Lasius niger (black garden ant) is the most ubiquitous ant in Amsterdam, nesting in pavement cracks, wall bases, and garden soil across the city, and observed on a diverse set of host trees including Laburnum, Styphnolobium, Taxodium, Thuja, Tilia and Zelkova. Workers forage across paved and unpaved surfaces, making them a genuine dual-habitat insect: ground-urban for their typical pavement-foraging and nest-siting, ground-park for green space foraging. Urban-walker is the dominant movement (workers patrolling pavements), with park-roamer for grass and park foraging trails. The six-tree host list reflects their generalism rather than tree-canopy specificity, so an override from the insect default is correct.

This entry covers Lasius and Formica ants observed specifically on the extrafloral nectaries of Ailanthus altissima (tree of heaven) and Liquidambar styraciflua in Amsterdam. Both trees are widespread urban species — Ailanthus is abundant on disturbed urban ground and Liquidambar is increasingly planted as a climate-adaptive street tree. Ants exploit the sugar secretions on stems and leaf petioles within the canopy zone rather than at ground level, making tree-canopy the correct dominant habitat. Their crawling on bark and stems is below animation threshold, so idle-only applies. The override is needed because EFN-tending ants are canopy-anchored, not flower-visitors.

This entry captures agromyzid fly larvae and various micro-moth caterpillars that produce leaf mines in Laburnum foliage, visible as pale blotch or linear trails on the leaflets. Laburnum anagyroides and L. x watereri are planted as ornamental trees in Amsterdam parks and private gardens, and leaf-miners frequently exploit them. As with all leaf-mining insects, larvae are entirely immobile within the mine tissue and idle-only is definitive. The override from the insect default correctly places them in tree-canopy rather than flower-visitor.

These hawthorn-feeding Lepidoptera caterpillars (lappet, lackey, vapourer, light emerald) live and feed directly on the foliage and bark of Crataegus trees growing in Amsterdam parks and street plantings. The larvae cling to branches and leaves, barely moving once settled on a feeding site, making idle-only the correct animation class. Tree-canopy is dominant because most species feed on foliage; tree-bark is added as secondary because egg batches and resting pupae are anchored to the bark surface. In Amsterdam these species are occasional visitors on ornamental hawthorn rows, most visible in spring and early summer when the caterpillars are active.

This catch-all entry covers rare or incidental geometrid loopers and generalist caterpillars observed on Ginkgo and other Amsterdam street trees. These larvae are leaf-miners or free-feeding defoliators that spend nearly all their larval life anchored to foliage, inching very slowly and spending long periods motionless — hence idle-only movement. Tree-canopy is the sole habitat because the caterpillars feed in the leaf layer rather than on bark. In Amsterdam Ginkgo biloba is a popular ornamental street tree and records of loopers on it are rare and opportunistic.

This entry covers four caterpillar species observed on Corylus, Fagus, Fraxinus and Nyssa in Amsterdam parks: Colocasia coryli (Nut-tree Tussock), Geometra papilionaria (Large Emerald), Plagodis pulveraria (Barred Umber) and Asthena albulata (Small White Wave). All four are foliage-feeding larvae that cling to leaves or rest motionlessly on branches during the day, perfectly matching tree-canopy and idle-only. Their cryptic resting behaviour means they are essentially static sprites in the canopy. The override from the insect default is required because these are caterpillar-stage defoliators, not flower-visitors.

The European hare (Lepus europaeus) and roe deer (Capreolus capreolus) are occasional browsers on young laburnum shoots and bark in Amsterdam's larger green areas such as the Amsterdamse Bos. Both are ground-dwelling mammals that range through open parkland and semi-wild green zones in search of woody browse. The park-roamer movement class fits perfectly: they walk and pause within vegetated polygons, pausing to feed. Roe deer are rare inside the ring-road but hares are regularly recorded in the Bos and city-edge parkland.

Lobarion and Graphidion are epiphytic lichen communities that colonise the smooth to furrowed bark of mature Fraxinus (ash) and other broadleaved trees in damp, well-lit urban parks. In Amsterdam these crusts and foliose thalli are found on older park trees with high air-quality scores, particularly in quieter canal-side and cemetery settings. They are entirely static — no movement whatsoever — and fully bound to the bark surface where they grow. The lichen category default of tree-bark + none fits perfectly and no override is needed.

The hawthorn leaf beetle is a small chrysomelid that feeds exclusively on Crataegus foliage both as larva and adult, making tree-canopy its clear dominant habitat in Amsterdam's park and street hawthorn plantings. Adults are slow walkers across leaf surfaces; they sit motionless for long periods and do not take meaningful positional flight between trees often enough to warrant tree-flitter. Idle-only correctly captures this almost stationary leaf-surface existence. Populations in Amsterdam track closely with ornamental hawthorn availability and are most visible from late spring through summer.

The Common Crossbill is an irruptive finch that descends on Amsterdam in invasion years to exploit the cone crops of Larix and Pinus in the Amsterdamse Bos and Vondelpark conifers. It spends virtually all its time in the upper canopy, twisting cones open with its crossed bill, and moves between nearby conifers in short darting flights — a classic tree-flitter pattern. The default bird tags of tree-canopy and tree-flitter are an excellent fit. Crossbills are irregular visitors: absent in most years, then suddenly present in noisy flocks when continental cone crops fail.

The Eurasian otter (Lutra lutra) and water vole (Arvicola amphibius) are semi-aquatic mammals strongly tied to Amsterdam's canals, ditches, and waterway edges lined with alder. Otters hunt in the water body itself but emerge and patrol along canal banks, while water voles build burrows in earthen water-edges and forage at the bank. Water-edge is dominant for placement since both are most visually present at the bank interface; water-body is added because otters swim and hunt below the surface. Movement alternates between bank-patrolling (water-edge-stalker) and in-water swimming (water-drifter).

The spotted lanternfly is an invasive planthopper native to Asia that feeds on phloem sap of Ailanthus altissima (tree of heaven) and other woody plants. In areas where it has established, nymphs and adults spend most time pressed flat against bark surfaces sucking sap, only briefly hopping to new positions. Tree-bark is dominant since this is where they congregate in mass; tree-canopy is secondary for adults that feed on foliage tips. Idle-only matches their characteristic motionless sap-feeding posture. Not yet established in Amsterdam but listed here as a potential invasive on the city's Ailanthus street trees.

Gypsy moth larvae are voracious defoliators of Ostrya and many other broadleaved trees, feeding openly on leaves in the tree canopy during the day and descending to the bark to hide at night. In Amsterdam the species is a notifiable pest; outbreaks on hop-hornbeam are rare but significant defoliation events. Tree-canopy is dominant because feeding and most visible life stages occur in the foliage; tree-bark is secondary for the nocturnal bark-resting behaviour. The caterpillars barely move during feeding bouts — idle-only correctly captures this suspended-on-leaves animation.

Hawkmoths such as the hummingbird hawk-moth (Macroglossum stellatarum) and other sphingids are powerful hovering pollinators that visit the trumpet flowers of Catalpa and other nectar-rich plants in Amsterdam gardens and parks. While feeding they hover motionless at blooms — flower-bobber — before making fast direct transit flights — sky-looper — between flower patches. Flower-visitor is the dominant habitat because their activity is tightly coupled to blooms; tree-canopy is the secondary resting habitat for adults that roost by day in foliage. In Amsterdam they are mainly summer migrants and passage visitors, most frequent in warm years.

The honey locust leafhopper is a specialist sap-sucker on Gleditsia triacanthos foliage, a tree increasingly planted as a street tree in Amsterdam's newer districts. Adults and nymphs sit on the undersides of leaflets, piercing phloem and barely moving during long feeding bouts. Tree-canopy is the sole relevant habitat since the entire life cycle plays out in the leaf layer. Idle-only fits because leafhoppers are virtually motionless while feeding, only making small sideways jumps when disturbed — below the threshold for a distinct movement animation.

The forest tent caterpillar is a North American lasiocampid whose Dutch presence is limited to ornamental Nyssa (tupelo) plantings; it would be an introduced curiosity rather than an established pest. Like other Malacosoma species, the larvae feed communally in foliage and rest on bark in silken mats. Tree-canopy is dominant for the feeding stage; tree-bark is secondary for the resting silk mats. The caterpillars are slow-moving leaf-feeders best represented by idle-only. Any Amsterdam observation would be exceptional and likely on an isolated ornamental tree.

The lackey moth is a native European lasiocampid whose gregarious caterpillars spin communal silk tents on Cornus, Prunus, and other woody shrubs and trees found in Amsterdam parks and hedgerows. Larvae feed on foliage and are most conspicuous as large silken colonies on branches — tree-canopy dominant — but the egg rings and resting clusters wrap bark — tree-bark secondary. Adult moths are nocturnal and short-lived; the conspicuous life stage is the caterpillar, which barely migrates from its feeding branch, making idle-only the right animation class.

The pine marten is a highly agile arboreal mustelid that climbs and leaps through the canopy of Pinus and Sorbus in woodland parks, using branches as travel routes and hunting sites. In Amsterdam it is an extreme rarity; occasional records come from the Amsterdamse Bos where mature pines exist. Tree-canopy is the dominant habitat because martens spend more time aloft than on the ground; ground-park is secondary for nightly terrestrial hunting forays. Movement alternates between tree-flitter (leaping between canopy trees) and park-roamer (ground-level hunting at night).

This invasive bruchid seed weevil develops entirely inside the pods and seeds of Gleditsia triacanthos, completing its larval stage concealed within the bean-like fruits hanging in the tree canopy. Adults emerge in summer, mate on the pods, and lay eggs — all without leaving the tree crown. Tree-canopy is the sole relevant habitat since the insect's visible stages (adults on pods) occur there. Idle-only fits because adults are slow-moving and spend most time resting on pods rather than flying. The species is now widespread in Dutch cities wherever honey-locust is planted as a street tree.

The Asian giant resin bee is the largest megachilid in Europe, an invasive species now established in the Netherlands that collects resin and pollen from flowering trees including Styphnolobium japonicum (Japanese pagoda tree), commonly planted as a street tree in Amsterdam. Like other leafcutter bees it is a dedicated flower-visitor and its movement between blooms is best represented by flower-bobber. Its large size makes it conspicuous while hovering at flowers. It nests in pre-existing cavities in wood or walls, but its observable activity is entirely at the flower level.

Leafcutter bees (Megachile) are solitary bees that visit flowers for nectar and pollen while also cutting neat discs from leaves — notably from Cercis siliquastrum (Judas tree) in Amsterdam parks and city gardens — to line their nest cells. They are strongly tied to flowering plants as their food source, making flower-visitor the dominant and sufficient habitat class. The flower-bobber movement class captures their characteristic short bobbing flights between blooms. In Amsterdam leafcutter bees are common visitors to garden flowers and the ornamental Cercis trees that bloom in late spring.

The Eurasian badger is a nocturnal omnivore that digs setts in earthen banks and forages across parkland and woodland edges for earthworms, roots, and fruit — including fallen Prunus plums and cherries. In Amsterdam badgers occur at the city fringe, particularly in the Amsterdamse Bos where sett systems persist in the sandy wooded banks. Ground-park is the right habitat since their entire above-ground activity is on vegetated soil. Park-roamer fits their slow nocturnal shuffle across park polygons, pausing frequently to root in the ground.

These phoretic mesostigmatid mites ride on bark beetles (particularly Ips typographus) that breed under the bark of Picea (Norway spruce) and are carried passively into new galleries when their beetle host colonises a new tree. Their own movement is negligible — they cling to the beetle's body or walk slowly within the bark gallery — making idle-only the correct animation. Tree-bark is the dominant habitat because the entire life cycle occurs in the subcortical space. In Amsterdam this mite community appears wherever spruce bark beetles attack stressed ornamental spruces.

The lime hawk-moth is one of Amsterdam's most familiar sphingids, whose large green caterpillars feed exclusively on the foliage of Tilia (lime/linden) — the dominant street tree of the city. Larvae live and feed openly in the canopy, hanging from twigs in a characteristic oblique posture and barely moving during feeding bouts. Tree-canopy is the correct dominant habitat; no secondary is needed since even resting adults settle in tree foliage or nearby walls. Idle-only captures both the near-motionless caterpillar and the perched adult moth; the moth's nocturnal flight is short-lived and not the primary visible state.

The hazel dormouse is a highly arboreal small mammal that climbs and forages among the branches of Corylus, Crataegus, and Prunus in Amsterdam's wooded parks, rarely descending to the ground except during autumn nut-gathering or hibernation. Tree-canopy is the dominant habitat because dormice move primarily through the shrub and tree layer; ground-park is secondary for ground-level foraging in autumn. Movement alternates between nimble canopy hops between branches (tree-flitter) and brief ground foraging bouts (park-roamer). The dormouse is a red-listed species in the Netherlands and any Amsterdam record would be exceptional, most likely from the Amsterdamse Bos.

Myzocallis carpini is a specialist aphid that forms colonies on the undersides of Carpinus betulus (hornbeam) leaves throughout the growing season. Hornbeam is common in Amsterdam parks and cemeteries as both a street tree and a hedge plant. The aphids are essentially immobile during feeding — apterae cling to leaf veins and exude honeydew — making idle-only the only appropriate movement class. Tree-canopy is the sole habitat since the colony lives entirely within the leaf layer. Winged alates disperse briefly in summer but this flight is invisible at the pixel-art scale.

Cherry blackfly (Myzus cerasi) forms dense, conspicuous black colonies on the curled shoot tips and leaf undersides of Prunus trees — ornamental cherries are among the most widely planted street trees in Amsterdam. The aphids remain stationary during feeding, with apterae virtually welded to the plant tissue, justifying idle-only movement. Tree-canopy is the dominant habitat because all feeding and colony growth happens in the upper leaf and shoot layer. The colonies are most spectacular in May–June on ornamental cherry avenues throughout the city, before natural enemies reduce populations.

Nematinae sawflies — especially the Pontania gallers — induce distinctive bean-like leaf galls on Salix (willow) species growing along Amsterdam's canals and in wetland parks. The larvae develop entirely enclosed within the gall tissue, making them invisible and completely immobile at the sprite scale; adults are short-lived and rarely observed on the tree. Tree-canopy is the sole habitat since the galls form on leaves. Idle-only is correct: the most visible sign of these insects is the static gall on the leaf surface, which barely changes day to day.

Nematus tibialis is a tenthredinid sawfly whose larvae feed gregariously on Robinia pseudoacacia (black locust) foliage, a tree widely planted on Amsterdam's sandy soils and disturbed sites. The larvae cluster on leaflets, consuming them from the margins inward, and barely move between feeding bouts — idle-only is the right animation. Tree-canopy is the dominant and sufficient habitat; no bark or ground stage is visible or relevant at this scale. Outbreaks can cause dramatic defoliation on Amsterdam's Robinia rows, making the species conspicuous to city residents in summer.

Magnolia scale is one of the largest soft scales in Europe and a conspicuous pest of Magnolia trees in Amsterdam parks and botanical collections, forming glossy dome-shaped brown bodies directly on the bark of twigs and branches. The settled scale insects are entirely immobile — they fuse to the bark surface and feed by suctioning phloem sap through a fixed stylet. Tree-bark is the dominant habitat since the scales live on the woody stem surface rather than the leaf layer. No movement animation beyond idle-only is appropriate. The species is readily identified on ornamental magnolias in summer by the distinctive waxy bumps and honeydew drip beneath.

The noctule is the largest common bat in the Netherlands and a classic tree-hole roosting species, using cavities in mature Betula and other deciduous trees in Amsterdam's parks and cemeteries. By day it roosts motionless in tree holes (idle-only, tree-canopy); at dusk it launches into high fast aerial foraging circuits over parks and open water (sky-looper, sky-only). Tree-canopy is dominant for placement because that is where the roosting colony is anchored; sky-only is secondary for the foraging flight. Noctules are often the first bats visible each evening in Amsterdam, flying high before dark.

This entry covers bat species (Noctule, Leisler's, various Myotis) that roost in cavities and crevices of mature Castanea and other veteran trees in Amsterdam parks, emerging at dusk to hunt insects over open water and parkland. Tree-canopy is the dominant habitat placement because the roost is in the tree; sky-only is added for the nightly foraging flights over open ground and water. Sky-looper is the primary movement class — bats follow long curved aerial paths — with idle-only as secondary for the daytime roosting state hidden inside the tree. Amsterdam's park trees, especially older ones, host significant bat roost populations.

The Camberwell Beauty is a large, striking nymphalid butterfly whose larvae feed gregariously on Celtis (hackberry) and Salix foliage, while adults are long-lived and frequently visit willow catkins and tree sap for nourishment rather than flowers. In Amsterdam it is a scarce but regular autumn migrant and occasional breeder on riverside willows. Tree-canopy is the dominant habitat because larvae feed in foliage and adults bask and roost on tree bark and branches; flower-visitor is secondary for adults seeking nectar and sap sources. Movement alternates between gliding flights between canopy trees (tree-flitter) and brief landings at food sources (flower-bobber).

The black locust gall midge is a tiny cecidomyiid fly whose larvae induce characteristic leaf-roll galls on Robinia pseudoacacia, an ornamental street tree found across Amsterdam. The adult midges and their larvae spend their entire life cycle within the folded leaf margins of the host canopy, never visiting flowers or foraging on the ground. Default insect tags (flower-visitor, flower-bobber) are overridden because this species is strictly a tree-canopy dweller with no flower-foraging behavior. The larval stage is entirely sedentary inside the gall, making idle-only the correct movement class.

This entry covers the assemblage of geometrid caterpillars — primarily winter moth but also related species — recorded on Ostrya carpinifolia (hop-hornbeam) in Amsterdam. These caterpillars feed in the leaf canopy during spring and early summer, moving only slowly between feeding sites. They have no flower-foraging role and do not visit the ground during their larval phase, making the default insect tags inappropriate. Idle-only is correct because caterpillars spend most daylight hours stationary, relying on camouflage rather than movement for survival.

Winter moth and mottled umber caterpillars are early-spring defoliators observed across a wide range of Amsterdam street trees including Amelanchier, Malus, Platanus, Prunus, Pyrus, and Quercus. The larvae hatch in sync with bud burst and feed exclusively in the canopy, looping slowly between leaves but never descending to the ground to forage. The default insect category tags (flower-visitor, flower-bobber) do not apply since these are foliage-feeding caterpillars, not pollinators. Idle-only captures the near-stationary caterpillar posture during the day, when they rest motionless on leaves to avoid predation.

The brimstone moth (Opisthograptis luteolata) and grey dagger moth (Acronicta psi) are both recorded as caterpillars on Amelanchier and Prunus in Amsterdam. Their larvae feed on foliage in the tree canopy throughout spring and summer, making tree-canopy the accurate placement habitat. Neither species is a flower-forager in its caterpillar stage, so the default insect tags are overridden. Adult moths are nocturnal and short-lived; the larval stage that appears in the observations is sedentary, justifying idle-only movement.

Orthosia moths (Common Quaker, Hebrew Character, and their relatives in the family Noctuidae) are early-spring moths recorded on Salix in Amsterdam. Their caterpillars feed on the fresh foliage of willows and other broadleaved trees in the canopy during April and May. Adults are nocturnal and rest motionless on bark or foliage by day, while caterpillars also remain largely still while feeding. The default flower-visitor/flower-bobber tags do not apply to these foliage-feeding noctuids, so tree-canopy and idle-only are the correct overrides.

Mason bees (Osmia) and mining bees (Andrena) are solitary bees that are conspicuous spring pollinators in Amsterdam, observed on Cercis and Prunus blossoms. They forage energetically between flowers, collecting pollen and nectar for their underground or cavity nests. The default insect tags of flower-visitor and flower-bobber are a perfect fit — these bees are quintessential flower visitors that bob between blooms in season. Outside the flowering window they are absent from view as adults, consistent with the flower-visitor fallback behavior.

Hackberry psyllids are tiny jumping plant lice (family Psyllidae) that induce nipple galls, blister galls, and bud galls on Celtis (hackberry) trees in Amsterdam. Their entire feeding and reproductive cycle is spent within the leaf tissue of the host canopy, making tree-canopy the only appropriate habitat class. Unlike flower-foraging insects, psyllids are phloem-feeders firmly anchored to their gall structures and rarely move once settled. Idle-only captures this stationary lifestyle; the default insect flower-visitor/flower-bobber tags are not applicable.

The large walnut aphid is a conspicuous, bright-yellow aphid that colonises the upper sides of walnut (Juglans) leaves in Amsterdam from spring to autumn. Colonies cluster on leaf veins and midribs in the canopy, feeding on phloem sap without ever descending to flowers or ground. The default insect tags (flower-visitor, flower-bobber) are completely inapplicable to this obligate tree-canopy phloem-feeder. Individuals within a colony barely move, making idle-only the correct animation class; winged dispersal forms are brief and infrequent.

Paracolopha morrisoni is a specialist gall-forming aphid that creates leaf-fold galls on Zelkova serrata, an increasingly popular Amsterdam street tree. The foundress and her colony spend the season inside the protective gall on the leaf, with no external movement visible. Zelkova does not produce the floral resources that flower-foraging insects seek, confirming that the default insect tags are unsuitable. Tree-canopy placement and idle-only movement correctly represent this highly sedentary, host-specific phloem-feeder.

Parectopa robiniella and the closely related Macrosaccus robiniella are North American leafminer moths introduced to Europe; their larvae create distinctive upper-surface blotch mines in Robinia pseudoacacia leaves across Amsterdam parks and streets. The tiny caterpillars are confined inside the leaf tissue throughout their larval development and cannot be seen without close inspection. There is no flower-visiting or ground-foraging phase for the larval stage captured in tree observations, so the default insect tags are overridden to tree-canopy and idle-only. Adults are small enough that their brief flights go unrecorded in the tree dataset.

This entry groups the core tit assemblage — blue tit (Cyanistes caeruleus), great tit (Parus major), and long-tailed tit (Aegithalos caudatus) — observed together on Crataegus in Amsterdam parks and gardens. All three species are archetypal canopy insectivores that move in restless mixed flocks, hopping and flitting between branches and trees throughout the year. The default bird tags of tree-canopy and tree-flitter capture this behavior precisely. Long-tailed tits also use hedgerow-style shrubs, but Crataegus qualifies as tree-canopy placement in the taxonomy.

The European fruit lecanium scale is a soft scale insect (family Coccidae) that encrusts the twigs and small branches of Taxodium and many other trees in Amsterdam. Adult females are permanently fixed to the bark surface under their waxy scale covering, making tree-bark the precise habitat class rather than tree-canopy. There is no flower-visiting, no ground presence, and no meaningful movement at any life stage visible in the tree dataset. Idle-only is the only appropriate movement class for this bark-anchored, entirely sedentary scale insect.

Great tit and blue tit are among the most familiar birds in Amsterdam, observed year-round in parks, gardens, and street trees including Juglans. They forage actively through the canopy for caterpillars, insects, and seeds, moving frequently between nearby trees with short arcing flights. The default bird tags — tree-canopy habitat and tree-flitter movement — are a perfect fit for these small, agile canopy birds. Both species readily use nest boxes in Amsterdam, but their visible behavior is canopy-foraging and inter-tree flitting.

This entry represents the mixed observation of cavity-nesting tits (Parus) and roosting pipistrelle bats (Pipistrellus) associated with hollow sections of Pterocarya (Caucasian wingnut) trees in Amsterdam. The observation context is the roost or nest cavity rather than active foraging, so idle-only better captures the roosting/resting state than the default tree-flitter. Bird is used as the dominant category since the taxon group is recorded primarily under avian observations; the bat component is a secondary occupant of the same cavities. Tree-canopy placement snaps correctly to the host tree where the cavity is located.

House sparrows and Eurasian tree sparrows are both recorded on Ginkgo in Amsterdam, but their key characteristic is their tight association with the built environment — nesting in wall cavities and roof spaces, then foraging on pavements and squares below. Wall-and-roof is the dominant habitat because both sparrow species are closely tied to buildings for shelter and nesting; ground-urban captures their pavement-foraging behavior. Urban-walker is the appropriate movement class, as both species hop along streets and squares picking up seeds and crumbs rather than flitting between trees. The default bird tags are overridden for this urban-specialist pair.

This entry records house sparrows and wood pigeons using Catalpa and Paulownia trees in Amsterdam specifically as roost sites, rather than in active foraging. Both species are common Amsterdam birds that spend the night roosting in trees, remaining motionless in the canopy for long periods. The tree-canopy habitat is correct for roosting birds perched in the crown, but the typical movement default (tree-flitter) is replaced with idle-only to reflect the roosting context of this observation. Wood pigeon and house sparrow differ in their active-phase habitats, but both roost in canopy foliage.

This entry covers the combined observation of house sparrows and great tits associated with Koelreuteria (golden rain tree) in Amsterdam. House sparrows are quintessential urban birds nesting in building crevices and foraging on pavements, while great tits forage in the canopy — the two species thus pull the habitat in different directions. Wall-and-roof is listed as dominant because house sparrow is the more characteristically urban component and is the species that gives this slug its name; tree-canopy is added as the secondary. Urban-walker suits the sparrow's pavement-foraging, and tree-flitter suits the tit, giving a two-value movement array.

Pauesia anatolica and related braconid parasitoid wasps are specialist parasitoids of aphids living on cedar (Cedrus) trees in Amsterdam. The adult wasps search the canopy for aphid colonies in which to lay their eggs, spending their active time within the tree canopy rather than visiting flowers. While adults have short flight bursts between searching bouts, the observation context is the parasitised aphid colony on the host tree. Tree-canopy and idle-only are the correct overrides, as these wasps are not flower-foragers but tree-canopy hunters whose presence is only detectable by the mummified aphids they leave behind.

The coal tit is a small conifer-specialist tit that regularly forages in the needle canopy of cedars (Cedrus), false cypresses (Chamaecyparis), dawn redwood (Metasequoia), spruces (Picea), and pines (Pinus) in Amsterdam parks. It is an agile canopy bird that flits quickly between branches and adjacent trees, often in mixed tit flocks during winter. The default bird tags — tree-canopy and tree-flitter — are a precise fit for this small, highly active songbird. Coal tits also cache seeds in bark crevices but their primary visible behavior is canopy foraging and inter-tree movement.

Phloeosinus aubei is a small scolytine bark beetle whose adults and larvae bore beneath the bark of Metasequoia (dawn redwood) and cypress-family trees in Amsterdam. Both the egg galleries and larval tunnels are entirely within the cambium and inner bark, making tree-bark the correct placement habitat. The beetle's presence is detected by its exit holes and frass rather than by visible surface activity, confirming idle-only as the movement class. The default insect tags (flower-visitor, flower-bobber) have no relevance to this bark-boring specialist.

This Metasequoia-specialist bark beetle (Curculionidae: Scolytinae) is a close relative of Phloeosinus aubei that bores into the bark of dawn redwood trees in Amsterdam. Like all scolytine bark beetles, it spends its entire life cycle — from egg laying through larval gallery construction to pupation — inside the bark, with only brief surface exposure during dispersal flights. Tree-bark is the sole relevant habitat class, and idle-only represents the typical invisible-in-bark lifestyle captured by tree observations. The default insect category tags are overridden for this obligate bark-dwelling species.

The woolly beech aphid is a distinctive species whose white waxy secretions make colonies immediately visible on the undersides of beech (Fagus) leaves in Amsterdam parks. Dense colonies cover the leaves from spring until early summer, feeding on phloem sap without any ground-foraging or flower-visiting behavior. Tree-canopy is the precise habitat since colonies are restricted to the foliage of Fagus, and idle-only reflects the sedentary clustered lifestyle of the apterous colony stages. The default flower-visitor and flower-bobber tags are completely inapplicable to this specialist tree-canopy aphid.

Phyllobius intrusus is a leaf-notching weevil recorded on Thuja (arborvitae) in Amsterdam, where adults feed on the scale-like foliage in the canopy. Adults are slow-moving, cryptic beetles that cling to foliage and move only short distances when disturbed. Unlike the default insect category of flower-visitors, this species is a foliage-feeder with no pollinator role, so tree-canopy and idle-only are the correct tags. Adults drop to the ground to overwinter in the soil, but their observable phase on Thuja is canopy-based.

Phyllonorycter esperella and similar gracillariid leafminers are recorded on Carpinus betulus (hornbeam) in Amsterdam. Their larvae live inside the hornbeam leaf, forming the blotch mines that identify this family, remaining completely enclosed within the leaf tissue until pupation. The default flower-visitor and flower-bobber insect tags have no relevance to these concealed leafminers, so tree-canopy and idle-only are used instead. Carpinus-associated leafminers are often among the most abundant insects by biomass on hornbeam street trees, though individual larvae are tiny and invisible without leaf-dissection.

This entry covers the assemblage of leafminer micro-moths (Phyllonorycter and relatives) observed on Castanea (sweet chestnut) in Amsterdam. Their larvae mine inside the leaf lamina, creating characteristic blotch or tentiform mines visible by holding leaves to the light; the caterpillar is entirely hidden within the leaf throughout its development. There is no flower-visiting stage recorded in this tree-associated observation, making tree-canopy and idle-only the appropriate overrides. Adults are tiny night-flying moths whose brief adult lives are not captured in tree-dataset observations.

Phytomyza ilicis is the holly leaf miner fly, whose larvae create the distinctive yellow-brown blotch mines visible on Ilex (holly) leaves throughout Amsterdam. The single larva develops entirely inside the leaf between the upper and lower epidermis, making its habitat strictly tree-canopy by placement. Adults are tiny, inconspicuous flies that mate and oviposit quickly in spring; the larval mines are the persistent visible evidence. The default insect tags are overridden to tree-canopy and idle-only, as this species has no flower-foraging role and the larval stage — the observable one — does not move.

Phytophthora lateralis is an oomycete root and crown pathogen that infects and kills Chamaecyparis (false cypress) trees in Amsterdam by rotting the roots and basal stem tissue. Although oomycetes are not true fungi, they are assigned to the fungus category as the closest practical equivalent in the taxonomy. The default fungus tag of tree-bark is overridden to tree-rooted because this pathogen operates belowground and at the root collar, not on the aerial bark. Movement is none, as all Phytophthora propagules move only with water flow and have no animated life stage.

Phytoptus avellanae, known as the big bud mite or hazelnut gall mite, infests the buds and developing shoots of Corylus (hazel) in Amsterdam, causing the characteristic abnormally swollen "big bud" symptom. These eriophyid mites are microscopic and spend their lives inside buds or beneath bud scales in the tree canopy, never descending to the ground or visiting flowers. The default arachnid tags (anywhere, idle-only) are partially overridden: idle-only stays, but anywhere is replaced with tree-canopy for precise placement on the host Corylus. The mites' entire ecology is bound to the aerial parts of their host tree.

Magpie (Pica pica) and carrion crow (Corvus corone) are two of Amsterdam's most successful urban corvids, observed together on Koelreuteria (golden rain tree) and across the entire city. Both species forage omnivorous on pavements, lawns, and rubbish, while also using trees as perch and nest sites — making anywhere the correct dominant habitat as specified for corvids in the taxonomy. The two-value movement array (tree-flitter, urban-walker) reflects their genuine alternation between tree perching with inter-tree flights and ground foraging walks on urban surfaces. This is the canonical example of the anywhere/multi-movement pattern from the taxonomy guide.

This catch-all grouping covers woodpeckers, nuthatches, and other cavity-excavating birds alongside small bats that roost in the same hollows in Amsterdam's urban trees. Woodpeckers are active on bark surfaces and in the canopy of large trees in parks like Vondelpark and the Amsterdamse Bos, while bats emerge at dusk to use hollows for daytime roost. The dominant tag is tree-canopy for animated placement, with tree-bark as secondary because woodpeckers cling vertically on trunks. Movement is primarily tree-flitter for the avian component, with idle-only capturing roosting bats that sit entirely still during daylight.

This pairing covers two bat species recorded roosting in the same Fagus tree in Amsterdam: the Common Pipistrelle and the larger Noctule, both of which use hollow branches and bark crevices as daytime roosts. The Noctule is one of Europe's highest-flying bats, often hunting above tree-canopy level well into open sky, while the Pipistrelle takes lower circuits; both share the sky-looper animation at dusk. Placement snaps to the tree bark during idle hours and transitions to sky-only during active flight. The mammal default does not fit either species, requiring a full override.

The Common Pipistrelle is Amsterdam's most frequently recorded bat, roosting by day in tight bark crevices of street trees such as Platanus, Populus, and Acer before emerging at twilight to hunt midges and moths. During active hours it flies fast looping circuits at 2–10 m above canals, parks, and lit streets — a textbook sky-looper. The tree-bark habitat tag places the sprite at a trunk during daylight with an idle-only animation, while the sky-only secondary and sky-looper movement dominate the dusk render. The mammal default of ground-park / park-roamer is entirely wrong for a bat, hence the override.

The Brown Long-eared Bat is a slow, highly manoeuvrable hunter that gleans insects from leaves and bark surfaces in wooded Amsterdam parks, while the Common Pipistrelle favours open aerial hunting; both species roost in Fraxinus cavities and bark crevices. Their shared tree-bark placement during daylight hours captures the roost site accurately, with sky-only as the active-flight habitat. The sky-looper animation is the dominant movement for the rendered dusk phase, and idle-only covers the stationary daytime roost. Both species override the mammal default of ground-park and park-roamer.

Roosting bats in Amsterdam use bark crevices and cavities of mature urban trees — Aesculus, Quercus, Tilia, and others — as daytime shelters, making tree-bark the correct resting placement. At dusk they leave to hunt insects on long curved aerial paths above canals, parks, and open water, which drives the sky-only secondary and the dominant sky-looper animation. The idle-only secondary captures the daytime roosting phase when no movement occurs. The mammal default of ground-park / park-roamer does not apply to any bat species, so this is a full override.

Prociphilus species form waxy woolly colonies on the roots and bark of Fraxinus, while Aphis fabae feeds on young shoots and leaves in the canopy of the same tree in Amsterdam street plantings. Both are sedentary once settled, forming dense stationary colonies rather than flying between flowers, so the insect default of flower-visitor / flower-bobber does not apply. Tree-canopy is dominant because the most visible infestations coat new growth and leaf undersides, with tree-bark as secondary for the bark-root colonies. Movement is idle-only: aphid movement is imperceptible at map scale.

Mealybugs are soft-bodied scale insects that settle in bark crevices and leaf axils of Catalpa, forming waxy white colonies that are essentially immobile once established. In Amsterdam they are occasional warm-season pests on ornamental Catalpa street trees, visible as woolly clusters on younger branches and bark. Because they feed anchored to the bark surface and do not visit flowers, the insect default of flower-visitor / flower-bobber is entirely wrong. Tree-bark placement and idle-only movement correctly represent a sessile bark-sucking insect.

Pseudoscorpions are tiny arachnids (1–5 mm) that live concealed in the deep bark furrows and under loose bark flakes of large urban trees such as Parrotia in Amsterdam, hunting mites and springtails within their micro-habitat. They are cryptic and virtually stationary from the perspective of any map-scale animation, sitting motionless in bark crevices for most of their lives. The arachnid default of anywhere / idle-only already captures their movement correctly, but the habitat needs an override from anywhere to tree-bark, which more precisely reflects where they are found. Idle-only movement remains unchanged.

The Rose-ringed Parakeet is now an established Amsterdam bird, with a growing population centred on parks with mature Platanus and other large trees where it excavates or usurps cavity roosts. It forages almost entirely in the canopy — feeding on buds, fruits, and seeds — and moves noisily between trees in small groups, matching the tree-flitter movement exactly. In Amsterdam it is most conspicuous in Vondelpark, Sloterpark, and Beatrixpark. The bird default of tree-canopy / tree-flitter fits without modification.

Barklice (Psocoptera) and bark-dwelling springtails (Collembola) are millimetric arthropods that graze algae, fungal spores, and organic films on the outer bark of Taxus and other urban trees in Amsterdam. They are among the most abundant but least visible bark micro-fauna, forming thin drifts visible only under magnification. Their movement is imperceptible at map scale and they never visit flowers, making the insect default of flower-visitor / flower-bobber inappropriate. Tree-bark placement with idle-only movement is the correct override for sedentary bark-grazing microarthropods.

Bagworm moths spend most of their larval life encased in a silk-and-debris bag anchored to the foliage or bark of conifers such as Thuja in Amsterdam gardens and parks. The bag-carrying larva moves very slowly along branches and twigs, effectively appearing stationary at map scale, while the wingless adult female never leaves her case at all. Tree-canopy is the dominant placement because bags are most visible hanging from leafy branchlets; tree-bark is secondary for larvae descending to pupate. The insect default of flower-visitor / flower-bobber does not apply to a sessile bark-and-foliage feeder, so idle-only is the correct movement override.

Pterocallis alni is a specialist aphid restricted to the undersides of Alnus leaves in Amsterdam canal-side and park plantings, where colonies feed on phloem sap from spring through summer. Like all tree-canopy aphids, colonies are sessile once settled — individuals may walk short distances between leaves but this movement is invisible at map scale. The secondary tree-bark tag covers the bark-overwintering phase when eggs are laid on Alnus twigs in autumn. The insect default of flower-visitor / flower-bobber does not fit a phloem-sucking leaf aphid, requiring an override to tree-canopy and idle-only.

Pulvinaria regalis is a soft scale insect that coats the bark of Aesculus (horse chestnut) and Platanus in Amsterdam streets with conspicuous waxy white egg masses in early summer. Adults and nymphs are glued to the bark surface beneath their waxy shield for virtually their entire lives, crawling only millimetres as first-instar crawlers before permanently settling. Bark is the exclusive habitat and movement is below map-scale perception, so tree-bark / idle-only is the precise override. The insect default of flower-visitor / flower-bobber has no relevance to a bark-feeding scale insect.

The Bullfinch is a stocky, shy finch that visits Amsterdam parks and tree-lined streets in autumn and winter, feeding on buds, berries, and seeds of Fraxinus, Sorbus, and Ulmus. It moves quietly through the mid- to upper canopy, typically in pairs or small family groups, flitting deliberately between trees rather than making long open-air crossings. In Amsterdam it is a scarce but regular winter visitor in places like Vondelpark and the Amsterdamse Bos wherever fruiting Sorbus or seeding ash are present. The bird default of tree-canopy / tree-flitter fits the Bullfinch without modification.

This qualitative grouping covers various moth and butterfly larvae that can feed on Styphnolobium japonicum (Japanese pagoda tree), a relatively toxic leguminous street tree in Amsterdam that most herbivores avoid. The caterpillars feed in the canopy foliage and move slowly along branches — imperceptible at map scale — making idle-only the correct movement class. Tree-canopy is the exclusive habitat because these are foliage-feeding larvae with no association with flowers or bark surfaces. The insect default of flower-visitor / flower-bobber does not fit leaf-feeding caterpillars, requiring an override.

The Brown rat is Amsterdam's most abundant and widespread urban mammal, foraging nightly along canal sides, under street furniture, and in pavement gaps throughout the city. Ground-urban is dominant because the bulk of activity and visibility is on paved surfaces near food sources — cafés, markets, bins, and canal banks — while water-edge is secondary because Amsterdam's canal network provides ideal burrowing banks and foraging margins. Urban-walker fits the short-distance scurrying along streets and alleys, with park-roamer as secondary for rats that range into park green. The mammal default of ground-park / park-roamer understates the urban pavement dominance for this species.

The Firecrest is a scarce but regular winter visitor to Amsterdam, favouring dense evergreen plantings of Chamaecyparis and similar conifers in parks and cemetery gardens. Like its close relative the Goldcrest, it is an energetic needle-gleaner that barely pauses between tree canopies, perfectly fitting the tree-flitter animation. It is smaller than a Blue Tit and even more hyperactive, flicking through foliage at short intervals and rarely descending to ground level. The bird default of tree-canopy / tree-flitter accurately represents this species without modification.

The Goldcrest is Europe's smallest bird and a conifer specialist strongly associated with Cedrus, Chamaecyparis, Metasequoia, Picea, Taxus, and Thuja in Amsterdam parks and private gardens. It forages ceaselessly through dense needle canopies hunting micro-insects and spiders, making rapid short flits between branches every few seconds. Amsterdam records show it present year-round in mature conifer stands in Vondelpark, the Hortus Botanicus, and larger private gardens. The bird default of tree-canopy / tree-flitter is an exact fit for this species; no override is needed.

This grouping covers two very small birds — the Goldcrest and the Coal Tit — that are closely associated with conifers, particularly Larix in Amsterdam parks. Both species are restless canopy foragers, working through needle clusters hunting tiny insects and spiders, and making short flitting hops between branches and nearby trees at high frequency. The Coal Tit joins mixed tit flocks in autumn and winter, while the Goldcrest is an active year-round conifer specialist; both are well served by the bird default of tree-canopy / tree-flitter without any modification.

The dogwood gall midge lays its eggs in the leaf margins and shoot tips of Cornus, inducing distinctive pod-like leaf-roll galls in the canopy of ornamental Cornus trees used in Amsterdam street plantings and parks. The larvae develop entirely within the gall tissue and are immobile; the adult midges are tiny and short-lived. Tree-canopy is the correct placement because galls form on foliage, and idle-only captures the effectively stationary appearance of gall-bound larvae. The insect default of flower-visitor / flower-bobber does not apply to a gall-inducing leaf miner, requiring the override.

The Walnut husk fly is a specialist fruit fly whose larvae develop inside the green husk of Juglans nuts, staining the kernel black and rendering it inedible. In Amsterdam it has established on ornamental and productive walnut trees, with adults resting cryptically on foliage in the canopy and larvae spending their entire feeding phase inside the ripening fruit. Adults do occasionally visit flowers for nectar but their defining ecological role is canopy-based oviposition on ripening fruit, so tree-canopy and idle-only better represent the animal than the insect default of flower-visitor / flower-bobber.

This grouping covers two moth species associated with Ilex aquifolium in Amsterdam: Rhopobota naevana (holly tortrix), whose larvae spin and roll holly leaves in the canopy, and Acasis viretata (yellow-barred brindle), a geometrid whose caterpillars feed on holly flowers and foliage. Both are canopy-bound in their larval stage and rest cryptically on foliage as adults, barely moving at map scale. They are leaf- and flower-tissue feeders rather than flower visitors in the pollinator sense, so the insect default of flower-visitor / flower-bobber does not apply. Tree-canopy and idle-only are the correct override.

Rhynchaenus fagi is a tiny jumping weevil (2–3 mm) whose larvae mine blister-like galleries in beech leaves, producing characteristic brown blotches visible from below Fagus canopies in Amsterdam parks such as the Amsterdamse Bos. Adults overwinter in leaf litter and ascend to the canopy in spring to feed and oviposit; their distinctive leaf-mines persist through summer. The entire life cycle is canopy-centred on Fagus foliage, with no association with flowers or open ground, making tree-canopy / idle-only the correct override from the insect default of flower-visitor / flower-bobber.

This grouping represents the mixed assemblage of small songbirds — tits, finches, sparrows, and thrushes — that use dense Cupressocyparis hedges and conifers in Amsterdam gardens as communal winter roosts after dark. During roosting hours the birds are entirely still, huddled in the foliage for warmth, so idle-only replaces the usual tree-flitter movement. Tree-canopy placement in a nearby evergreen is correct for a roosting group; the birds are not flitting but are genuinely present and stationary in the canopy. This is a nocturnal / crepuscular phenomenon, so the override from tree-flitter to idle-only is necessary.

The White-letter Hairstreak is a specialist butterfly almost entirely dependent on Ulmus, whose larvae feed on elm flowers and young leaves in the canopy in spring. Adults spend most of their time resting on elm foliage and visiting aphid honeydew high in the canopy, rarely descending, so tree-canopy is the dominant habitat. Flower-visitor is a secondary because adults do take nectar from bramble, privet, and other flowers when these are accessible near elms. Movement is primarily idle-only (perching motionless for long periods on leaves) with flower-bobber as a secondary for the brief nectaring excursions — a different profile from the insect default of flower-visitor / flower-bobber alone.

The Eastern Grey Squirrel has established itself in Amsterdam parks, particularly in areas with mature Quercus, Platanus, Aesculus, and Ulmus, where it is now the dominant squirrel in some locations. It is highly arboreal, leaping confidently between canopies and descending frequently to bury and retrieve food caches in park lawns, making tree-canopy dominant and ground-park secondary. Tree-flitter fits the arboreal phase well, and park-roamer captures the characteristic stop-start ground foraging. The mammal default of ground-park / park-roamer alone misses the equal arboreal importance; both habitat and movement need a two-value override.

This entry records squirrel activity in a single Ginkgo tree in Amsterdam, likely Grey Squirrel given the current Amsterdam population distribution. Squirrels use Ginkgo for its fruit (the nut inside the malodorous outer seed coat) in autumn, foraging in the canopy and on the ground beneath the tree. Tree-canopy is dominant as the feeding and travel phase; ground-park is secondary for the nut-caching and retrieval on park lawns. Tree-flitter and park-roamer cover the respective movement phases. The mammal default of ground-park / park-roamer alone omits the arboreal component, requiring the same override applied to other squirrel entries.

This pairing covers both the native Eurasian Red Squirrel (now very rare in Amsterdam) and the introduced Grey Squirrel, observed together in stands of Juglans, Picea, Pinus, and Pterocarya. Both species share an essentially identical ecology for placement purposes: dominant time in the canopy leaping between conifers and large deciduous trees, with regular descents to cache and retrieve nuts on park ground. Tree-canopy is dominant and ground-park secondary for habitat; tree-flitter and park-roamer cover the two movement phases. The mammal default of ground-park alone does not capture the strongly arboreal lifestyle, requiring the override.

This pairing groups grey squirrels with bank voles, both recorded in the same diverse tree stands (Castanea, Cedrus, Nyssa, Taxus, and others) in Amsterdam parks and wooded areas. Squirrels are the dominant visual element, spending much of their time in the canopy leaping between branches, with frequent descents to the ground to cache food — hence tree-canopy dominant and ground-park secondary. Bank voles are ground-dwelling and nocturnal, better captured by the park-roamer secondary. Tree-flitter is the dominant animation for the squirrel aerial phase, and park-roamer covers both vole ground movement and squirrel foraging on the grass. The mammal default of ground-park / park-roamer alone misses the arboreal component, requiring an override.

Elm bark beetles bore directly into the bark and cambium of Ulmus trees, spending almost their entire adult and larval life beneath or on the bark surface rather than visiting flowers. In Amsterdam they are associated with the many surviving elm street trees, where the galleries they carve under the bark are the primary sign of their presence. They are also the main vector of Dutch elm disease fungus, making them ecologically significant beyond their feeding habits. The insect default of flower-visitor / flower-bobber does not apply; tree-bark captures their anchor point and idle-only reflects the minimal surface movement between gallery entrances.

The hornet moth is a clearwing moth whose larvae tunnel deep into the base and roots of Populus trees, spending two to three years inside the wood before the adult emerges. Adults are mimics of hornets and rest cryptically on bark or low stems near their emergence holes; they do not visit flowers in any meaningful way. In Amsterdam poplars are widespread along canal banks and park edges, giving the hornet moth a consistent set of host trees. Tree-bark is the correct habitat class because the larval galleries and adult emergence are both anchored to the trunk surface; idle-only reflects the adult's tendency to rest still on bark rather than move actively.

Both the Eurasian nuthatch and the great spotted woodpecker forage almost exclusively on bark and woody stems, probing crevices and excavating for invertebrates rather than flitting between canopy foliage like a typical songbird. They are recorded on the Corylus and other mature deciduous trees in Amsterdam's parks such as Vondelpark and the Amsterdamse Bos. The bird default of tree-canopy / tree-flitter is wrong for these bark-specialists; tree-bark places them on the trunk and larger branches where they actually operate. Idle-only captures their characteristic freeze-in-place posture on vertical bark rather than any continuous locomotion.

This composite entry covers the small mammals most commonly associated with Morus and other fruiting trees in Amsterdam parks: urban foxes foraging beneath them, grey squirrels and red squirrels climbing into the canopy for fruit, and wood mice gleaning fallen mast from the ground. The dominant habitat is ground-park because foxes and mice are predominantly ground-level; a secondary tree-canopy tag captures the squirrel component that genuinely climbs. Park-roamer is the right movement class for the fox and mouse element and adequately represents the group; squirrel acrobatics can be folded into the same category given the small sprite scale.

The caterpillars of both the eyed hawk-moth and the puss moth feed openly on willow and poplar foliage, clinging to leaves and shoots in the tree canopy throughout their larval phase. Adult eyed hawk-moths and puss moths are night-flying and rest motionless on bark or foliage during the day, neither visiting flowers nor moving actively in daylight. Salix trees along Amsterdam's canal banks provide abundant larval habitat. Tree-canopy is correct because the larvae spend their entire feeding period in the foliage; idle-only reflects the cryptic resting posture of both adults and older larvae.

The pine hawk-moth lays its eggs on Pinus needles and the caterpillars feed gregariously in the canopy, perfectly camouflaged against the bundled needles. Adults are nocturnal and spend the day pressed flat against pine bark, resting invisibly. Amsterdam's ornamental and planted pines in parks such as Amsterdamse Bos provide the larval host; the species is uncommon but recorded. Tree-canopy is correct for the larval feeding stage, which dominates the observable part of the life cycle; idle-only reflects both the motionless daytime adult resting on bark and the slow deliberate feeding of the caterpillar.

The Eurasian siskin is an acrobatic finch that feeds primarily on the seed catkins of alder and birch, hanging from thin outer twigs while extracting seeds; it also uses larch and spruce in Amsterdam's planted groves. Flocks move between trees through the canopy in the classic flitting pattern of small passerines, making tree-canopy and tree-flitter the correct defaults. In Amsterdam siskins are most reliably seen in winter and early spring when alder and birch catkins are plentiful along park waterways. The bird default fits without any override needed.

The ash weevil feeds on the buds and young leaves of Fraxinus in spring, and the larvae develop inside the leaflets where they cause characteristic leaf-roll damage. Adults are small and cryptic, sitting still on ash foliage or bark rather than visiting flowers. Amsterdam has many ash street trees and park specimens, and the weevil is regularly recorded on them. Tree-canopy replaces the insect default of flower-visitor because the entire life cycle is tied to ash foliage; idle-only replaces flower-bobber because the weevil's movement is negligible at the scale of a map sprite.

Stigmella microtheriella is a tiny nepticulid moth whose larvae mine the leaves of Carpinus (hornbeam), creating narrow sinuous galleries visible as pale trails through the leaf blade. The adult moths are minute, nocturnal, and rest motionless on leaf surfaces or bark during the day. Hornbeam is well-represented in Amsterdam parks and avenues, providing reliable habitat. Tree-canopy is the right class because the leaf-mining habit keeps the organism entirely in the foliage layer; idle-only reflects both the motionless adult and the slow creeping of the leaf-bound larva.

Stomaphis yanonis is an unusually large bark-feeding aphid that inserts its long stylet into the phloem of Zelkova bark rather than feeding on leaf tissue; colonies aggregate on the trunk and major branches beneath ant tents. Zelkova is a planted street and park tree in Amsterdam, and this aphid is an uncommon but notable associate. Tree-bark is correct because the colonies are anchored to the trunk surface rather than the canopy foliage; idle-only reflects the completely sessile colony life of phloem-feeding Stomaphis. A photo of a colony on Zelkova bark showing the ants tending them would best represent this species.

This composite entry groups cavity-nesting and bark-associated birds that use Fraxinus and other mature trees with natural hollows or decayed sections in Amsterdam parks. The tawny owl roosts and nests in tree cavities, largely motionless by day; the common redstart perches in canopy foliage and sallies for insects; the nuthatch and woodpeckers work the bark surface for invertebrates. Tree-canopy is dominant because the canopy-perching and cavity-entrance activity is most visible; tree-bark is the secondary because nuthatch and woodpecker spend real time on trunk surfaces. Idle-only is dominant reflecting the owl's daytime roost; tree-flitter captures the redstart's active flitting between foliage perches.

The European starling is one of Amsterdam's most abundant urban birds, foraging in noisy flocks on lawns, pavements, and squares while also roosting and breeding in tree cavities and roof spaces. It visits fruiting Morus and Nyssa trees to gorge on berries, giving it a genuine tree-canopy secondary, but the dominant activity visible on streets and parks is ground foraging. Ground-urban is the primary habitat because pavement and lawn foraging is what observers see most; urban-walker captures the characteristic striding gait, with tree-flitter as secondary for the berry-feeding and roost-approach flights.

Wild boar root for mast beneath Fagus and Robinia in forested park areas, turning over the soil surface with their snouts in classic park-roamer fashion. In Amsterdam they are occasional visitors to the edges of the Amsterdamse Bos from the surrounding polder landscape rather than permanent residents, but their association with beech mast makes the park setting correct. Ground-park and park-roamer are both accurate defaults for a large root-foraging mammal. The species is unlikely to be encountered in the inner city but is plausible in larger green zones recorded in the iNaturalist dataset.
